html:
Start here

The TRIALMON Dashboard is the deterministic safety-monitoring backbone of an early-phase trial, made visible. It takes the same auditable rules that the validated %tm_* monitoring job runs every load — freshness gates, screening bands, the eDISH AND-gate, the 3+3 state machine — and renders them as one self-contained, offline, click-through picture so a biostatistician or medical monitor can see where the screen is pointing before they pick up the phone. It is a teaching and illustration view. It is not a console, not a record, and not a place a reported number ever comes from.

The one rule, before anything else. Every colored cell, pill, chip and flag on this dashboard is a screening prompt for a human — never a determination, diagnosis, adjudication, or reportable number. A RED tile means “a person should look at this now,” not “this is a finding.” The medical monitor, the Safety Review Committee (SRC), pharmacovigilance, and the covering biostatistician decide. The dashboard only shows you what to look at first.

What this dashboard is

It visualizes a single synthetic study, CP-101: a 22-subject, five-cohort (C1–C5, 10/30/60/120/200 mg, N=3/3/6/6/4) Phase 1 dose-escalation trial run on a classic 3+3 rhythm across eight nominal visits (Screen, C1D1, C1D8, C1D15, C2D1, C2D8, C3D1, EOT). The underlying data is longitudinal — every subject has a per-visit series for each safety parameter — and it is seeded (SEED 20260611, a mulberry32 generator), so the picture is bit-for-bit reproducible every time the file is opened. Nothing is random at view time.

What the dashboard derives is deliberately tiny and fully auditable: the treatment-emergent AE (TEAE) flag, and the per-cohort 3+3 state (a trigger / decision-pending label). Everything else — relationship, seriousness, expectedness, DLT adjudication, the screening bands themselves — is either an investigator/SRC/sponsor-recorded assessment that the dashboard merely displays, or a deterministic band computed by a published rule. It never invents a causality call and never declares an MTD.

A key concept: the validated backbone, made visible. The numbers and flags here mirror what the validated monitoring job already computes; the dashboard adds pixels, not logic. That is why a hard backward-compatibility invariant holds throughout: each per-visit series’ worst on-treatment value equals the original summary value, so the new per-visit panels can never contradict the headline eDISH or QTcF plots. See the FAQ for how to confirm that yourself.

What this dashboard is NOT

  • Not a source of reported numbers. Incidence tables, the ICH E14 categorical table, central-read QT intervals, concentration–QTc analysis, AE incidence, the MTD, and final disposition come only from validated tools — Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline. Any count panel here that resembles a regulatory table is explicitly labelled “screening census — not the validated table.”
  • Not an adjudication engine. It will show you a subject sitting in the potential Hy’s-Law quadrant; it will never call that subject “a Hy’s-Law case.” See Governance for the approved language.
  • Not an interpretation engine. This is the deterministic, validated-backbone view only. It screens against pre-specified univariate thresholds and shows you what crossed; it does not attempt a model-based or multi-system “gestalt” judgement about whether several co-occurring sub-threshold values add up to something — that judgement belongs to the human reviewer, not the screen.
  • Not a record and not PHI. The data is synthetic and illustrative — no real subjects, no unblinding, no randomization or kit codes, no identity-narrowing fields, no free-text narratives, and no Grade-5/death events.

Why a field guide

The dashboard is dense on purpose, and a colored cell carries a lot of governance behind it. This wiki is the reference manual that sits next to it — the document that tells you not just what a panel shows, but what it is allowed to mean, which silences are intentional, and where the validated number actually lives. It is written at operating-wiki depth so that a medical monitor or covering biostatistician can read a section cold and act correctly.

It is also built to be read in more than one way — skim the callouts, read a section end-to-end, or jump straight to a subject’s evidence packet — because retention comes from being able to approach the same material from whichever direction fits the moment.

Offline, self-contained, how to open it

The dashboard is a single HTML file. The charts are hand-rolled SVG; there are no external libraries, no fonts to fetch, no analytics, no network calls of any kind. To open it: double-click the file, or drag it into any modern browser (Chrome, Edge, Firefox, Safari). It renders fully air-gapped — on a locked-down workstation, on a plane, inside a validated environment — and no data ever leaves the file. There is nothing to install and nothing to configure.

Practical: because the data is seeded, the file you open today and the file a colleague opens next quarter show the identical picture. That makes it safe to reference a specific subject or cell in an email or a training session — the coordinates are stable.

How this wiki is organized

Read top to bottom the first time; after that, jump by need.

SectionWhat you’ll find
A 60-second tourThe first screen, walked through in order — status band, KPI strip, sub-nav, the attention worklist, and click-to-drill.
GovernanceThe non-negotiable rules: screening-not-determination, approved Hy’s-Law language, severity vs seriousness, where validated numbers live.
Data & provenanceThe synthetic CP-101 design, the seed, the per-visit model, and the backward-compat invariant.
OverviewKPI tiles, the attention worklist, the data-freshness/SLA panel, and the study heatmap.
HepaticeDISH with history trails, LFT trajectories, the R-ratio strip, and the peak/baseline table.
CardiacQTcF (Fridericia) trajectories and the ICH E14 categorical screening census.
Labs & vitalsThe baseline→worst lab-shift grid, hematology and renal panels, and the orthostatic/PCS board.
AE & DLTThe 3+3 escalation ladder, the SOC×grade AE matrix, and the AE onset/duration swimlane.
DispositionThe enrollment funnel, RBQM KRI tiles, the deviation log, and the visit-compliance matrix.
SubjectsThe sortable roster and the per-subject evidence-packet modal.
Planted signalsThe honest-by-design teaching cases — including the AND-gate near-miss and the multivariate-only subject.
From dashboard to productionHow this view relates to the validated %tm_* job and the real pipeline.
ReferenceThresholds, formulas (Fridericia, CKD-EPI 2021, R-ratio), and controlled vocabularies.
FAQThe questions a monitor actually asks — can I report a number, why is 011 silent, is this telling me it’s Hy’s Law, and more.

If you read only one more thing after this page, read Governance — it is the difference between using the dashboard correctly and over-reading it.

A 60-second tour

Open the file and you land on the Overview screen of a dark operations console. Everything you need to triage is on this one page, arranged top-to-bottom in the order a monitor actually reads it: is anything on fire right now (the status band), can I trust what I’m looking at (the freshness chips), what’s the shape of the study (the KPI strip), and then what specifically should I look at first (the attention worklist). This tour walks that path once, end to end.

The walk-through, in order

  1. The status band (top-left): the worst-of-all RAG pill. One pill summarizes the entire screen by taking the worst single state anywhere in the study — RED if any open RED flag exists, AMBER if the worst open item is amber, GREEN only if nothing is open. On CP-101 it reads RED, because subjects like CP101-014 (Hy’s-Law screening position) and CP101-018 (QTcF 503 ms) are open. The pill is a tripwire, not a verdict — it tells you the page is not quiet, then sends you down to find out why.
  2. The freshness chips (top-right): can I trust the green? Next to the status band sit the per-feed freshness chips — one per data feed, each showing its age against its SLA. This is the first thing to read, because of the cardinal rule: a panel can look GREEN on its content and still be escalated to RED if the feed it rests on is stale (“green-on-stale-data”). Pipeline freshness is a separate axis from data-content QC, and it is shown first-class for exactly that reason. See Overview for the SLA panel and load-tick history.
  3. The KPI tile strip: the shape of the study at a glance. Across the top sits the tile strip — enrolled count, open RED flags, open AMBER flags, serious AEs, adjudicated DLTs, enrollment %, and the data-feed state. This is your orientation: how big the study is, how much is open, and whether the feed underneath everything is healthy. The numbers here are screening counts, not reported figures — see the governance note on why an incidence figure never comes off a tile.
  4. The section sub-nav: the seven views. Below the KPIs is the sub-nav linking the seven deep sections — Overview, Hepatic, Cardiac, Labs & vitals, AE & DLT, Disposition, and Subjects — plus a clearly-fenced Exploratory ▸ structure tab (unsupervised analytics, not the validated backbone). You don’t need any of it yet; the Overview will route you.
  5. Your first stop: the Overview attention worklist. This is the heart of the triage. The worklist lists every open AMBER/RED screening flag, highest-severity first, each row click-to-open. It is intentionally not a list of everything interesting — watch-tier subjects and operational/multivariate-only subjects are deliberately excluded, and that silence is a teaching point, not an omission (more in Planted signals). Start at the top row and work down.
  6. The study heatmap: when did it start? Just below the worklist is the subject×visit heatmap — each cell colored by the worst deterministic screening severity at that visit. This is your “when did it start” view: scan a flagged subject’s row to see the visit the color first appears. A hatched cell is a missed/operational visit (route to a human, never “fine”); a baseline-watch cell is shown distinctly so a pre-existing finding isn’t mistaken for a drug effect.
  7. Two more Overview panels. The cohort signal ranking orders the dose cohorts by a composite of DLT / SAE / Gr≥3 counts — a look-here-first ordering for the SRC, never an escalation. And the scenario simulator is a what-if: pick a hypothetical overnight event (a feed failing, a 2nd DLT, a new QTcF 500 ms) and watch how the deterministic roll-up and paging would respond. It is purely illustrative and never changes the live state — it just teaches the routing logic.
  8. Click to drill into any subject. Click any worklist row, any heatmap cell, or any subject in the roster, and the evidence-packet modal opens: deterministic flags first, then the per-visit sparkline trajectories, then provenance. This is where you confirm the prompt with your own eyes before deciding anything.
The fastest correct path: read the freshness chips → glance at the status pill → drop into the attention worklist → click the top row → read its evidence packet. That sequence answers “can I trust this, is anything open, what’s worst, and is the prompt real” in under a minute — and it never asks you to over-read a single cell.
One honest caveat for the tour. The worklist showing a subject is the start of work, not the end of it. A RED row means “a human should look,” and the evidence packet helps that human look well — but the determination (is this Hy’s Law, is this related, is this serious, does this meet a DLT) is made by the medical monitor and SRC, never by the row turning red. Carry that into every click. See Governance.

Once you’ve done the loop once, the natural next read is Planted signals — it names the specific subjects this picture was built to teach, including the ones that are correctly silent — and then the Exploratory ▸ structure tab if you want the unsupervised, topological view of the same cohort.

FAQ

The questions a biostatistician or medical monitor actually asks in the first hour with this dashboard — answered plainly, with a pointer to the section that goes deeper.

Reading the numbers

Can I report a number off this dashboard?
No. Nothing here is a reportable or regulated figure. Incidence tables, the ICH E14 categorical table, central-read QT intervals, concentration–QTc, AE incidence, the MTD, and final disposition come only from validated tools — Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline. Any count panel that resembles a regulatory table is labelled “screening census — not the validated table” for exactly this reason. See Governance.

The cohort rates are based on 3–6 subjects — aren’t those unstable?
Yes, and that’s expected. With N=3 or N=6 per cohort, a single event swings the rate enormously, so the small-N rates carry an explicit instability caveat and should be read as direction, not estimate. They are a prompt to look at the underlying subjects, never a number to quote. See AE & DLT.

How do I know the per-visit data matches the summary plots?
By design: a hard backward-compatibility invariant holds — each per-visit series’ worst on-treatment value equals the original summary value. So a subject’s eDISH dot or QTcF peak is literally the worst point of its own trajectory; the per-visit panels can extend the story (when it started, the trail) but can never contradict the headline. You can confirm it on any subject by opening its evidence packet and checking the trajectory peak against the summary marker. See Data & provenance.

The flags and what they mean

Is the eDISH plot telling me a subject has Hy’s Law?
No — it is telling you a subject sits in the potential Hy’s-Law quadrant, an AND-gate position pending confirmation. Hy’s-Law logic is only entertained after a confirmatory repeat, an R-ratio/ALP cholestasis exclusion, and ruling out viral, biliary, alcohol, concomitant-drug, and underlying causes — all human work. The approved language never calls a subject or region “a Hy’s-Law case.” See Hepatic and Governance.

Why is subject CP101-011 not flagged anywhere?
Because it is the multivariate-only case: every univariate value sits below its own action threshold at every visit — QTcF 474 is WATCH not AMBER, ALT 2.6× is under the 3× band, Hgb/ANC are sub-Grade-2. The deterministic backbone is correct to stay silent. Recognising whether such coordinated, sub-threshold drift is worth a closer human look is a matter for the reviewer’s judgement, not something a univariate screen is built to flag. That silence is the teaching point, not a miss. CP101-012 is a milder version of the same co-drift. See Planted signals.

Then why is CP101-009 not in the worklist if its ALT is elevated?
Because it is the AND-gate near-miss: ALT 4.2× but TBili 1.6× (under 2×), so it is correctly GREEN on eDISH at every visit — the Temple’s-Corollary region of ALT-without-bilirubin. It carries a non-serious Grade-2 “ALT increased” AE but does not fire the AND-gate, so the worklist is right to be quiet on it. See Hepatic.

Subject CP101-018 shows QTcF 503 ms but a baseline of 462 — is that a drug effect?
The dashboard flags the 503 ms on-treatment value RED as a screening prompt, but it also shows the baseline 462 distinctly as a watch-band point — the subject was already above 450 ms before dosing. Whether the change is drug-related is a human call; the baseline-watch styling exists precisely so a pre-existing finding isn’t mistaken for a treatment effect. See Cardiac.

Does a Grade-3 screening band mean the AE is serious?
No. Severity (CTCAE grade) and seriousness (the ICH regulatory criteria) are kept strictly separate — a Grade-3 AE is not automatically serious. And the “grade” you see on a single lab value here is a deterministic screening band, not an adjudicated AE grade. Both calls belong to the investigator/SRC. See Governance and AE & DLT.

Freshness, completeness, and the pipeline

Why is a GREEN feed shown as RED?
Because the content can be clean while the pipeline is stale. If a feed is past its SLA, any GREEN built on it is escalated to RED — “green-on-stale-data” — since a healthy-looking panel resting on old data is exactly the failure mode that hides a real change. Pipeline freshness is a first-class axis, distinct from data-content QC. See Overview.

A subject has a missed visit — is that “fine”?
Never automatically. A missed visit (like CP101-003) is an operational data-completeness gap that can mean missed safety labs, so it is shown as hatched/operational and routed to a human — the dashboard will not color it GREEN and move on. See the visit-compliance matrix in Disposition.

What about the 3+3 ladder — does the dashboard decide to escalate or call the MTD?
No. It computes which 3+3 branch the adjudicated DLT count satisfies and shows a trigger / decision-pending label — for example C4 at 2/6 reads as an MTD-exceeded TRIGGER — but it never escalates, expands, de-escalates, or declares an MTD. The SRC decides. Note also that planned N is read from the design (C3 was designed N=6, never “expanded”). See AE & DLT.

The file itself

Can it run offline — does any data leave the file?
Yes, fully offline, and no. It is a single self-contained HTML file with hand-rolled SVG, no external libraries, no fonts to fetch, and no network calls of any kind. Double-click it or drag it into any modern browser; it works air-gapped and nothing leaves the page. See Start here.

Is any of this real subject data?
No. Everything is synthetic and seeded (SEED 20260611) — no PHI, no real subjects, no unblinding, no randomization or kit codes, no identity-narrowing fields, no free-text narratives, and no Grade-5/death events. It is built to be safe to share, screenshot, and teach from. See Data & provenance.

If you remember one answer from this page: every flag is a screening prompt for a human. The dashboard tells you where to look and helps you look well; the medical monitor, SRC, and pharmacovigilance make the determination. See Governance.

The Governance Model

This is the spine of the TRIALMON Dashboard, and the section to read before any other. The dashboard’s charts are persuasive — an eDISH scatter with a subject sitting in the upper-right quadrant looks like a finding. The discipline that makes this tool safe to put in front of a medical monitor is the set of hard rules below: what a flag is and is not, what language is permitted, where reportable numbers come from, and who actually decides. Every panel in Overview through Subjects inherits these rules. If a panel ever seems to break one, the panel is wrong, not the rule.

The one rule under all the others. The TRIALMON Dashboard is a screening instrument. It produces prompts for a human. It never produces a determination, a diagnosis, an adjudication, a causality assessment, a grade, an interval, or a reportable number. Everything below is an elaboration of that single sentence.

1. A flag is a screening prompt, not a determination

The deterministic backbone applies fixed, auditable thresholds to per-subject, per-visit values and raises a colored flag when a value crosses an action band. That is the entire claim. A flag says, in effect, “a human should look at this subject, at this domain, now.” It does not say the subject has a condition, that a drug caused anything, that an adverse event is serious, or that a dose level has failed.

The distinction is not pedantic. Early-phase safety decisions are made by people with regulatory authority and clinical judgment — the medical monitor, the Safety Review Committee (SRC), pharmacovigilance (PV), and, when the lead biostatistician is out, the covering biostatistician. The dashboard exists to make sure those people see the right subjects quickly and with full context. It does not exist to make their decisions, and it is constructed so that it cannot, even by accident, look like it has.

Screening vs. determination, made concrete. Compare two subjects from the planted teaching set. CP101-014 trips the hepatic eDISH AND-gate (ALT 5.6×, TBili 3.1×). The dashboard’s correct output is a RED screening flag reading “potential Hy’s-Law quadrant — route to medical monitor.” The dashboard does not say this subject is a Hy’s-Law case, because Hy’s Law is a clinical determination that requires confirmatory repeat testing, R-ratio / ALP review to exclude a cholestatic pattern, and exclusion of viral, biliary, alcohol, concomitant-drug, and underlying causes. The flag is the start of that workup, never its conclusion.

This is why the Attention Worklist is deliberately quiet about certain subjects. CP101-011 — the multivariate-only case — has every univariate value below its own action threshold at every visit (QTcF 474 ms is WATCH not AMBER; ALT 2.6× is under 3×; Hgb and ANC are sub-Grade-2). The deterministic backbone correctly never fires, so CP101-011 never appears on the worklist. That silence is intentional and is itself a teaching point: a screening tool screens against declared thresholds, and the honest thing to do when nothing crosses a threshold is to stay silent. Any “look harder” intuition about co-drifting values is a judgement for the human reviewer — not something this threshold-based screen asserts.

2. Approved Hy’s-Law language — say this, never that

Hepatic safety language is where well-meaning dashboards do the most damage. The phrase “Hy’s Law case” carries enormous regulatory weight: a single genuine Hy’s-Law case in a development program can change a drug’s trajectory. A screening tool must never apply that label, because the tool has not done — and cannot do — the confirmatory and differential-diagnostic work the label requires. We constrain the vocabulary explicitly.

Approved phrasings (and only these) for the AND-gate region.
  • “potential Hy’s-Law quadrant”
  • “Hy’s-Law screening position”
  • “AND-gate position, pending confirmation”
These all describe a position on a plot awaiting human work — never a property of the patient.
Say thisNever thisWhy
“CP101-014 is in the potential Hy’s-Law quadrant at C2D8.”“CP101-014 is a Hy’s-Law case.”Hy’s Law is a clinical determination requiring confirmation and exclusion of other causes; the plot position is not the determination.
“AND-gate position pending confirmatory repeat and cholestasis exclusion.”“Confirmed DILI” / “drug-induced liver injury detected.”Causality and the DILI diagnosis are physician judgments; the dashboard observes labs, not mechanism.
“Screening flag: ALT ≥3× ULN AND TBili ≥2× ULN co-occur.”“The drug is hepatotoxic at 120 mg.”An attribution to dose/drug requires the SRC’s review of the whole cohort, not one scatter point.
“R-ratio ≈4.0 suggests a MIXED pattern — review.”“Hepatocellular injury.”The R-ratio is a pattern prompt; the unstable-near-ULN guard and clinical context can overturn the naive label.
“Eligible for Hy’s-Law evaluation by the medical monitor.”“Meets Hy’s Law.”“Meets” implies the full rule (incl. excluding cholestasis and other causes) has been satisfied. It has not.
The near-miss is the proof the language matters. CP101-009 reaches ALT 4.2× ULN but TBili only 1.6× (under 2×). On the eDISH plot it sits in the right region (high ALT) but low on bilirubin — Temple’s Corollary territory, ALT elevation without bilirubin involvement. The AND-gate correctly stays GREEN at every visit, and CP101-009 stays off the worklist (it carries only a non-serious Grade-2 “ALT increased” AE). If the tool used loose language — “possible Hy’s Law” the moment ALT crossed a line — it would have manufactured a false alarm on a subject the AND-gate is specifically designed to not flag. Precise vocabulary is what makes the AND-gate trustworthy.

3. Reportable numbers come from validated tools — never from here

Several dashboard panels resemble tables that appear in a Clinical Study Report or a regulatory submission: an AE incidence matrix, a categorical QTc outlier census, a disposition funnel, cohort DLT counts. The resemblance is intentional — it makes the screening view legible to people who read those tables — but the numbers on the dashboard are not those numbers and must never be quoted as if they were.

Source-of-truth rule. Every reported, regulated, or submitted number originates only from validated tooling: Phoenix WinNonlin for PK and concentration-QTc, Pinnacle 21 for CDISC conformance, and the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline with its central-read intervals and adjudicated assessments. The TRIALMON Dashboard contributes zero reportable values. Any panel that mirrors a regulated table carries the standing label “SCREENING CENSUS — NOT THE VALIDATED TABLE.”

Concretely, the things you must take from the validated pipeline and never from this dashboard include:

  • the ICH E14 categorical QTc table — the Cardiac census is a screening tally, not the central-read E14 analysis;
  • AE incidence tables by SOC and grade — the AE matrix is a subject-incidence screening view of recorded events, not the locked analysis dataset;
  • the MTD and any escalation/expansion/de-escalation decision — see rule 6;
  • final disposition and completion/discontinuation tallies — the funnel is a live operational picture, not the database-lock disposition;
  • any PK exposure, central-read interval, or concentration-QTc relationship.
How to phrase it in a memo. Write “the screening view flags CP101-018 for QTcF in the >500 ms band; the categorical E14 counts will be confirmed from the central read.” Never write “we have 3 subjects with QTcF >500 ms” with the dashboard as your citation. The dashboard tells you where to look; the validated pipeline tells you what to report.

4. Severity and seriousness are kept strictly separate

This is the rule most often collapsed in casual conversation, and collapsing it has regulatory consequences. Severity (a CTCAE grade) describes the intensity of an event. Seriousness is a regulatory classification under ICH criteria — death, life-threatening, hospitalization or prolongation thereof, persistent/significant disability, congenital anomaly, or an important medical event. They are orthogonal. A Grade 3 event is not automatically serious; a Grade 2 event can be serious if it meets an ICH criterion.

Two axes, never merged. The dashboard displays severity (CTCAE grade) and seriousness (ICH SAE criteria) as independent attributes. It never infers one from the other, never colors a high grade as “serious,” and never colors a serious event as necessarily high-grade. The AE & DLT matrix keeps the grade axis and the seriousness attribute visibly distinct for exactly this reason.
“Grade” on a single lab value is a screening band, not an AE grade. When the lab-shift grid shows a value in a “CTCAE-style” band, that is a deterministic severity band applied to one number — a screening convenience so the picture reads in familiar units. It is not an adjudicated adverse-event grade. An adjudicated AE grade reflects the investigator’s clinical assessment of the whole event over time, not a single threshold crossing. We even cap the hemoglobin value-band at Grade 3, because a true clinical Grade 4 anemia call requires monitor assessment that a value alone cannot supply.

5. What the dashboard DERIVES vs. what it only DISPLAYS

Drawing this boundary precisely is what lets the tool be both useful and safe. The dashboard is allowed to compute a very small number of things, by fixed deterministic auditable rules. Everything else it shows — faithfully, with provenance — but never originates.

The dashboard DERIVES (deterministic, auditable)The dashboard only DISPLAYS (recorded elsewhere by a human/validated system)
The TEAE flag — whether an event is treatment-emergent, by comparing onset timing to first dose, a mechanical rule.Relationship / causality — the investigator’s drug-relatedness assessment. The dashboard never derives relatedness from timing or pattern.
The 3+3 state — given the recorded, adjudicated DLT count and the designed N, which 3+3 branch the count satisfies, expressed as a trigger or decision-pending label.Seriousness (SAE status) — the ICH regulatory classification, recorded by the investigator / PV.
The per-visit screening severity bands applied to clinical values (the deterministic threshold crossings that color the cells).DLT adjudication — whether a given toxicity counts as a dose-limiting toxicity. The SRC adjudicates; the dashboard reads the result.
The data-freshness / SLA state per feed (load recency vs. SLA) — see rule 7.Expectedness, the ICH E14 central read, AE incidence, PK exposure, MTD, and final disposition — all from validated systems.
The 3+3 ladder computes a trigger; it never makes a decision. The 3+3 state ladder may display, for cohort C4, “2 of 6 evaluable DLTs — MTD-exceeded TRIGGER.” That is an arithmetic statement about the recorded count against the rule. The dashboard does not escalate, expand, de-escalate, or declare an MTD. The SRC decides. And note the design fact the ladder reads straight from the protocol: C3 was designed N=6; it was never “expanded from 3.” The DLT observation window is 28 days, also read from the design, not inferred.
Why TEAE and 3+3 are the only derivations. Both are pure functions of recorded inputs with no clinical judgment: TEAE is a date comparison; the 3+3 branch is a lookup keyed on an already-adjudicated count and a fixed design. Neither asks the tool to assess a patient. The moment a computation would require judgment about a patient — is this related? is this serious? is this a DLT? — it leaves the dashboard’s authority and becomes a displayed, human-recorded value.

6. Causality, seriousness, expectedness, and DLT adjudication are inputs, not outputs

Expanding on the right-hand column above: four assessments in particular are recorded by people with the authority and the source documents to make them, and the dashboard’s only job is to surface them next to the screening context so a human can reason. The dashboard must never appear to generate them.

  • Relationship/causality is the investigator’s call. A tight cluster of AE onsets inside the DLT window (visible in the onset/duration swimlane) is how monitors reason about drug-relatedness — but the swimlane is a reasoning aid, not a verdict. The dashboard never writes “related.”
  • Seriousness is the regulatory SAE determination — PV / investigator, against ICH criteria.
  • Expectedness is assessed against the reference safety information — not something a screening threshold can know.
  • DLT adjudication is the SRC’s. For CP101-014 and CP101-013 (the two C4 DLTs) and CP101-010 (one DLT in C3), the dashboard shows the adjudicated DLT and lets the ladder count it — it did not decide it was a DLT.

7. Stale-green is escalated to RED: pipeline freshness ≠ content QC

A green panel built on a feed that has not loaded recently is more dangerous than a red one, because it broadcasts “all clear” from data that may already be wrong — the safety event may have happened and simply not arrived yet. TRIALMON treats this as a first-class failure mode, not a footnote.

The green-on-stale-data rule. If a feed’s load age exceeds its SLA, any GREEN content state riding on that feed is escalated to RED on the Data-Freshness / SLA panel. A clean screen does not earn trust unless the data under it is current.
Two independent questions. Pipeline freshness asks “is the data current?” — load recency vs. SLA, shown as per-feed age plus a load-tick history. Content QC asks “is the data, as of its last load, clean?” — the threshold crossings and screening flags. A feed can be perfectly clean and dangerously stale; the freshness panel is the only thing that catches that combination, which is why it sits on the Overview with first-class status alongside the KPI tiles.

8. Synthetic data, no PHI, no unblinding

This dashboard is a teaching and illustration artifact. The CP-101 trial, its 22 subjects, and every value are synthetic and seeded (SEED 20260611, mulberry32) so the picture is reproducible across builds. There are no real subjects and no protected health information anywhere in it.

Hard constraints on what the dashboard may contain.
  • No PHI and no real subjects — synthetic, illustrative data only.
  • No unblinding: no randomization codes, no kit codes, no identity-narrowing free-text fields.
  • No case-narrative prose — controlled vocabularies only, never a written clinical story.
  • No Grade-5 / death anywhere in the dataset.
  • Small-N cohorts (N=3–6) always carry the rate-instability caveat — a “2 of 6” is not a stable percentage and must be read as counts.
Why the no-narrative rule is load-bearing. A free-text narrative is where re-identification and inadvertent unblinding creep in, and where a screening tool would be most tempted to editorialize into a determination. By restricting every field to a controlled vocabulary, the dashboard physically cannot tell a story about a patient — it can only point. That is by design and it is consistent with rule 1.

9. Humans decide — the named roles

“Route to a human” is not a hand-wave. The roles are specific, and each owns a defined slice of judgment that the dashboard explicitly does not.

RoleOwns the decision the dashboard prompts
Medical monitorClinical interpretation of a screening flag — e.g., taking a potential Hy’s-Law quadrant position into a real hepatic workup (confirmatory repeat, cholestasis exclusion, differential diagnosis); making the Grade-4 anemia call the value-band deliberately withholds.
Safety Review Committee (SRC)DLT adjudication and all dose decisions — escalate, expand, de-escalate, declare MTD. The 3+3 ladder hands the SRC a trigger; the SRC decides.
Pharmacovigilance (PV)Seriousness (SAE) determination, expectedness, and regulatory reporting obligations — the ICH classifications the dashboard only displays.
Covering biostatisticianOperational continuity when the lead is out — triaging the worklist, confirming a flag is real vs. a freshness artifact, and escalating to the right role above. The dashboard is built so a covering statistician can pick it up cold and route correctly.
What this buys you. Because the dashboard never crosses into determination, a medical monitor can trust it without auditing every pixel: a flag is always “look here,” never “I’ve decided.” The worklist’s silences (like CP101-011) are honest, the hepatic language is precise enough to survive a regulatory reading, the reportable numbers still come from the validated pipeline, and a stale feed can never masquerade as good news. The tool earns its place precisely by knowing where it stops.

The synthetic data model

Everything in this dashboard is fabricated — deliberately, transparently, and reproducibly. There are no real subjects, no PHI, no unblinding, and no reported numbers. What you see is a seeded synthetic trial built to make the deterministic monitoring backbone visible and teachable. This section documents how the data is generated, what the longitudinal model contains, and the one hard invariant that keeps the new per-visit panels from ever contradicting the headline plots. Read it before you trust a single pixel: knowing the data is manufactured is the whole point, and the construction is itself the lesson.

Synthetic, by design — and labelled as such everywhere. The data is illustrative, not a record. No real subjects; no PHI; no randomization, kit, or IXRS codes; no identity-narrowing fields; no free-text case narratives (controlled vocabularies only); and no Grade 5 / no death — the most severe outcomes are intentionally excluded because they belong to medical-monitor adjudication, not a screening illustration. Small cohorts (N = 3–6) mean every rate carries an instability caveat; a 1/6 and a 2/6 are two people, not a percentage you should quote. Nothing here is a claim that any signal is real or robust.

Seeded and reproducible

The dashboard draws every random quantity from a single deterministic pseudo-random generator: a mulberry32 stream initialized with SEED = 20260611. Normal deviates are produced by a Box–Muller transform over that uniform stream (rn(mean, sd)), and bounded with explicit clamp() limits so no analyte ever wanders into the physically absurd. Because the seed is fixed and the generator is deterministic, the picture is identical on every open, in every browser, on every machine — the same subjects, the same trajectories, the same flags, forever. That reproducibility is not a convenience; it is what makes this a teaching artifact you can point two colleagues at and have them see exactly the same thing, and it mirrors the discipline a frozen, validated pipeline brings to a real read.

The 22 subjects are generated cohort by cohort from the design table C1–C5 at doses 10 / 30 / 60 / 120 / 200 mg with planned N = 3 / 3 / 6 / 6 / 4 — a classic 3+3 rhythm. Subject identifiers are assigned in enrollment order as CP101-001 through CP101-022. A per-subject toxicity scalar (tox = dose/200) gently raises the expected number of AEs, the SAE probability, and the Grade-3 probability with dose — so the synthetic trial has a plausible dose–toxicity gradient baked in, without any single value being hand-placed except the planted teaching cases.

The longitudinal (per-visit) model

Earlier versions of this view carried only a single worst-on-study summary value per subject. The current model is longitudinal: each subject has a scaffold of 8 nominal visits — Screen, C1D1, C1D8, C1D15, C2D1, C2D8, C3D1, EOT at target study days −7, 1, 8, 15, 22, 29, 43, 53 — and most clinical and operational series are realized at every visit. This is what powers the history trails, the LFT and QTcF trajectories, the lab-shift bands, the heatmap, and the per-subject sparklines in the evidence packet.

The visit scaffold itself is modeled, not idealized:

  • Windows and jitter. Early visits carry a tight ±1-day window; later visits a ±3-day window. The actual visit day is the target plus a small seeded jitter. If the realized day falls outside the window, the visit is marked out-of-window (oow) and surfaces in the visit-compliance matrix.
  • Missed visits. A visit can be flagged absent (present: false, status missed). CP101-003 misses C2D1 — an operational data-completeness gap that propagates as a null through every series, rendered as a hatched cell, never as a clinical severity.
  • Clinical series. Per visit, each subject carries hepatic LFTs (ALT / AST / TBili / ALP / GGT in ×ULN, plus a derived R-ratio and a recomputed AND-gate flag), an ECG record (QTcF by Fridericia, ΔQTcF, HR, PR, QRS, a tier, and a baseline-watch marker), a lab panel (Hgb, ANC, platelets, WBC, creatinine with a CKD-EPI-2021 race-free eGFR estimate, BUN, Na, K, corrected Ca), and a vitals/orthostatic record.
  • Event-level AEs. Adverse events are individual records, not just counts: each carries a MedDRA-style SOC and preferred term drawn from a fixed controlled vocabulary, a CTCAE grade, onset and resolution study days, a TEAE flag, an investigator-recorded seriousness flag and seriousness criterion, a recorded relationship/causality, and an expectedness flag. The planted high-severity constellations (e.g. CP101-014's related Grade-3 hepatobiliary cluster) are set explicitly; the rest are sampled, skewed toward Grade 1–2.
  • Disposition, feeds, deviations. Each subject has an enrollment day, a disposition status and recorded reason (C1–C2 completed; CP101-014 and CP101-013 discontinued for AE). Two screen-failures and a short protocol-deviation log use controlled vocabularies. Five pipeline feeds (adam.adlb, adam.adeg, adam.adae, safe.sae, ixrs.rand) carry an age in hours and an SLA — ixrs.rand is deliberately stale.

How the series are built — and the hard backward-compat invariant

Each per-visit series is constructed by interpolating from a baseline toward a single extreme, with the peak placed at a chosen visit and the remaining points filled either monotonically (for a clean rise) or with seeded scatter (for a noisier course). The extreme value handed to the series builder is exactly the subject's original summary value. This produces the non-negotiable invariant that governs the entire longitudinal layer:

The hard backward-compatibility invariant. For every per-visit series, the worst on-treatment value equals the original summary value (same SEED). The peak ALT in CP101-014's trajectory is 5.6× because the summary ALT was 5.6×; the highest QTcF in CP101-018's trace is 503 ms because the summary QTcF was 503 ms; the Hgb nadir equals the summary Hgb. Nothing in a trajectory can exceed, undershoot, or otherwise disagree with the headline value.

Why this matters. The signature plots a medical monitor reasons from — the eDISH scatter on Hepatic, the QTcF census on Cardiac, the AE matrix on AE & DLT — are anchored to the summary values. By forcing every new per-visit panel to peak at exactly that summary value, the longitudinal layer can add the "when did it start" story without ever introducing a contradiction. A history trail can show you the path a subject took to the eDISH corner, but the corner is in the same place it has always been. After the event-level AEs are assigned, the count rollups (nae, sae, g3) are re-derived from the events themselves, so the AE incidence panels and the worklist stay exact too. This is the same property a real validated pipeline must hold: a richer view of the data is only trustworthy if it cannot silently change the numbers a reviewer already acted on.

How to sanity-check the invariant yourself. Open any subject's evidence packet and read the sparkline peak against the headline value on the relevant domain page (Hepatic eDISH or Cardiac QTcF). They will match to the displayed precision. If they ever didn't, the longitudinal panels would have to be treated as a separate, untrusted source — the invariant is what lets them sit on the same page as the validated backbone.

Thresholds, SLAs, and windows are illustrative

The numeric cut-points wired into this dashboard are reasonable, conventional, and illustrative — they are not the live limits of any specific study. The eDISH AND-gate is set at (ALT or AST >3×ULN) AND (TBili >2×ULN); QTcF tiers at >450 (watch) / >480 (AMBER) / >500 (RED) and ΔQTcF at >30 / >60 ms; the R-ratio bands at ≥5 hepatocellular / 2–5 mixed / ≤2 cholestatic with an "unstable near ULN" guard when ALT or ALP sits below 1.2×; CTCAE-style screening bands come from a single analyte-metadata table (with Hgb capped at the Grade-3 band on purpose); the DLT window is a single fixed 28-day first-cycle window; visit windows are ±1 / ±3 days; and feed SLAs are 6–24 hours. In a live deployment, every one of these reads from the protocol and the SAP — the central-lab ULN of record, the protocol-defined DLT window and evaluability rules, the pre-specified QTc correction and outlier categories, the SAP's CTCAE version, the per-visit windows in the schedule of assessments, and the data-management SLAs. The dashboard's job is to apply the study's rules deterministically and make the result visible; the rules are not the dashboard's to invent.

Screening census, not the validated table. Any panel here that resembles a regulatory output — the ICH E14 categorical outlier counts on Cardiac, the AE incidence matrix on AE & DLT, the disposition funnel on Disposition — is a screening census on synthetic data, labelled as such. The reported, regulated numbers (incidence tables, the actual E14 table, central-read intervals, concentration–QTc, MTD, final disposition) come only from validated tools (Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline) — never from this view. See Governance for the full rule set and From illustration to production for how the live version is wired.

Guided cases (planted signals)

A monitoring dashboard is only as trustworthy as its behavior on the hard cases — and the hardest cases are the ones where the right answer is to stay quiet. This trial is seeded with ten teaching subjects, each engineered to exercise one specific reasoning step a medical monitor or covering biostatistician makes. Some of them light the board up; the two most important ones are correctly silent, and that silence is the lesson. Walk these cases and you will have walked the entire governance model in the form it actually shows up in: a real subject, a real-looking value, and a deliberate decision about whether the deterministic backbone should speak.

Honest-by-design. The point of a planted signal is not to make the dashboard look clever — it is to make its boundaries legible. The AND-gate near-miss (CP101-009) and the multivariate-only drift (CP101-011) are present specifically so you can confirm the worklist does not escalate them. If your eye expects an alert there and the board gives you nothing, the board is right and your univariate intuition needed the correction. That is the most valuable thing this view teaches.

The centerpiece: 014 vs 009 — the AND-gate

These two subjects exist to be read together. They are the reason the eDISH plot is a scatter and not two separate bar charts, and they are the single most important pair on the board.

CP101-014 — the gate fires. Peak ALT 5.6×ULN and peak TBili 3.1×ULN. Both limbs of the AND-gate are satisfied — (ALT >3×) AND (TBili >2×) — so the subject lands in the upper-right potential Hy’s-Law quadrant of the eDISH scatter and fires a RED screening flag at the visit where both cross. On the trajectory panels you can watch how it got there: ALT rises first and TBili lags by a visit (the bilirubin peak is placed one visit later than the transaminase peak), which is exactly the temporal pattern that makes hepatocellular injury with later hyperbilirubinaemia worrying. There is a deliberate clinical twist: the R-ratio is ≈4.0, which is a MIXED pattern, not classically hepatocellular — a reminder that the eDISH position and the R-ratio pattern are two different lenses and you read both. CP101-014 is also an adjudicated DLT, carries serious, related, unexpected hepatobiliary AEs (related Grade-3 ALT increased and hyperbilirubinaemia), and is discontinued for AE. It lights up the eDISH RED flag, the worklist, the SAE row, the DLT ladder, and the disposition funnel — the full chain.

Approved language, even on the case that fires. CP101-014 occupies a potential Hy’s-Law quadrant / a Hy’s-Law screening position / an AND-gate position pending confirmation. It is not a "Hy’s-Law case." The flag is a prompt to a human, and the AND-gate position is only the entry to a workup: confirmatory repeat, R-ratio / ALP cholestasis exclusion, and ruling out viral, biliary, alcohol, concomitant-drug, and underlying causes must come before Hy’s-Law logic is entertained. The dashboard displays the position; the medical monitor adjudicates the case. See Governance and Hepatic.

CP101-009 — the near-miss, and why one limb is not enough. Peak ALT 4.2×ULN — clearly elevated, well past 3×. But peak TBili is 1.6×, below the 2× limb. The AND-gate requires both, so CP101-009 sits in the lower-right of the eDISH scatter — the Temple’s-Corollary region: transaminase elevation without accompanying bilirubin — and is correctly GREEN on eDISH at every visit. This is the case that disciplines a too-eager eye. A reviewer scanning ALT alone would flag it; the AND-gate does not, because isolated transaminitis without hyperbilirubinaemia carries a very different prognosis than the combination Hy described.

The silence is the teaching point. CP101-009 has a real adverse event — a non-serious Grade-2 "ALT increased," possible relationship — and it still does not appear in the attention worklist. That is correct. The worklist surfaces open AMBER/RED screening flags; an isolated below-gate transaminase bump with a non-serious AE is neither. If you wanted to know about it, the AE is visible on the AE & DLT matrix and in the subject’s evidence packet — the data is never hidden, it is simply not escalated. A board that screamed about CP101-009 would be a board you would learn to ignore.

The other centerpiece: 011 — multivariate-only, correctly silent

CP101-011 is the subtlest and most important case on the board, because it is the one a univariate screen cannot catch — and is right not to. Every value drifts in the worrying direction, and every single one stays below its own action threshold at every visit:

  • QTcF 474 ms — this is the watch band (>450), not AMBER (which needs >480). No cardiac flag.
  • ALT 2.6×ULN — below the 3× eDISH limb, and TBili 1.7× below the 2× limb. No hepatic flag.
  • Hgb 11.1 g/dL and ANC 1.9 — sub-Grade-2 on the screening bands. No heme flag.
  • Creatinine 1.28 mg/dL — a modest fold-rise, not a threshold crossing.

So several organ systems are quietly drifting together — a pattern a thoughtful human might want to eyeball — yet no univariate rule is satisfied, and the deterministic backbone correctly does not fire. CP101-011 is absent from the worklist and shows as GREEN/watch across the heatmap.

This is the limit of univariate, threshold-based screening — and it is shown honestly. Coordinated multi-organ drift that stays below every individual threshold is, by construction, invisible to a screen that fires only when a pre-specified univariate rule is crossed. The dashboard does not pretend otherwise: it puts CP101-011 here, silent on the worklist, precisely so the reviewer can see that a clean backbone is not the same as a clean subject. Whether such a gestalt is worth a closer human look is a clinical judgement for the medical monitor / covering biostatistician — the deterministic backbone makes no such call, by design.

CP101-012 — the gradient. A milder version of the same idea: ALT 2.3×, TBili 1.4×, QTcF 468 ms (still watch, <480). Every value sub-threshold; GREEN/watch on every check; correctly silent. 012 sits next to 011 as the shallower point on the ambiguous-co-drift gradient — useful for seeing that "multivariate-only" is not a single magic case but a region the backbone deliberately leaves to human clinical judgement.

The cardiac pair: 018 and 006 — always read the baseline

CP101-018 records QTcF 503 ms at an on-treatment visit — over 500, a RED tier, and it appears in the worklist with a related/possible Grade-3 "Electrocardiogram QT prolonged" AE. But the baseline (screening) QTcF was already 462 ms, which is itself in the watch band. The trajectory panel and the baseline-watch marker make this explicit: a subject who starts above 450 and rises further is a different reasoning problem than one who starts normal and crosses. CP101-006 is the AMBER analogue: QTcF 486 ms (>480), baseline 453 already watch-band.

Baseline-watch is shown distinctly for a reason. A high on-treatment QTcF on a subject whose baseline was already elevated is not, on its face, a drug effect — it may be the subject’s own electrophysiology. The dashboard renders these baseline points distinctly (a separate marker, not a clinical tier) so the medical monitor reads the delta in context. The flag still fires — an as-collected QTcF over 500 is a prompt no matter the baseline — but the board hands you the baseline in the same glance so you do not over-read it. As always, the as-collected value is pending ECG QC and central read; see Cardiac.

The 3+3 pair: 013, 010, and how the trigger crosses

CP101-013 and CP101-014 are the two C4 DLTs. Two adjudicated DLTs in the 6-subject C4 cohort — 2/6 — satisfies the rule that exceeds the MTD, and the 3+3 state ladder shows C4 as an MTD-exceeded TRIGGER — SRC decision pending. CP101-010 is a single DLT in C3 — 1/6 — which lands C3 in the AMBER "1 DLT, expansion/rule check, SRC decides" branch. CP101-013’s recorded events include a serious, related, hospitalisation-criterion Grade-4 neutrophil decrease; CP101-010’s a Grade-3 platelet decrease.

The board computes the branch; it never decides. The dashboard derives which 3+3 branch the adjudicated DLT count satisfies — that is a deterministic, auditable arithmetic on counts the SRC already adjudicated — and labels it TRIGGER or decision-pending. It does not escalate, expand, de-escalate, or declare an MTD. The SRC decides. Note also that C3 and C4 were designed at N=6; C4 is not "expanded from 3." DLT relationship and adjudication are recorded assessments the dashboard only displays. See AE & DLT and Governance.

The operational pair: 003 and 020 — not everything that moves is clinical

CP101-003 — a missed visit. The subject misses C2D1. This is an operational data-completeness gap, not a clinical severity. It shows as a hatched/operational cell on the study heatmap and in the visit-compliance matrix, and as a Minor deviation in the log. The crucial teaching: a missed visit can mean missed safety labs, so it is routed to a human — it never means the subject is fine. The right response to a gap is to go get the data, not to assume green.

CP101-020 — a protocol dose change. A protocol-driven dose adjustment shifts several labs at one visit at once. Because all the shifted values stay sub-threshold, the subject is GREEN on every clinical check — but the simultaneous movement is logged as an operational deviation (dosing/IP). This is the operational mirror image of CP101-011: 011 is several values drifting together for an unknown reason (a pattern for a human to weigh, since no single rule crosses); 020 is several values shifting together for a known operational reason (a deviation to record, not a clinical signal). Telling those two apart is precisely the judgment a covering biostatistician brings, and the board lays out the evidence for both without pretending to make the call.

How to run the guided tour. Start on Overview and note who is — and is not — in the worklist (009 and 011 absent is the checkpoint). Open Hepatic and read 014 and 009 on the eDISH scatter side by side. Open Cardiac and read 018/006 with their baselines. Open AE & DLT and confirm the C4 trigger. Then click each subject for its evidence packet — deterministic flags first, then the sparkline trajectories that show the path, then provenance. By the end you will have exercised every rule in Governance on a concrete person.

Overview — the day-one triage screen

The Overview is the first screen you land on and, on a quiet morning, often the only one you need. It answers a single question for the covering biostatistician or medical monitor: where, if anywhere, does a human need to look today? Four panels do that job — a KPI tile strip that rolls the whole study into seven numbers, an attention worklist that lists every open action-tier screening flag in priority order, a first-class data-freshness / SLA panel that watches the pipeline itself, and a study heatmap that shows subject × visit so you can see when a signal started. Everything here is a roll-up of the same deterministic screening flags you see in the domain sections; nothing on this page is computed independently, so nothing here can quietly disagree with the detail below it.

The Overview is a derived view, never a new source. Every tile, worklist row, and heatmap cell traces back to a per-visit deterministic flag that already exists in Hepatic, Cardiac, Labs & Vitals, AE & DLT, or the freshness feed list. The single combining operator is worst() — a pure max over a fixed severity rank (GREEN/watch/baseline-watch = 0 · AMBER = 1 · RED = 2). Because the page only ever takes the worst of values that already exist, the headline can never be greener than the worst panel underneath it. This is the structural guarantee that makes a one-glance summary safe to trust.

The KPI tile strip

Seven tiles sit across the top of the screen. Each is a pure aggregation — a count or a worst() roll-up — with a tiny caption that names the regulatory or operational concept it stands for. Read left to right, they take you from how much trial there is to what is on fire to is the data even current.

TileWhat it rolls upColour / jump

Why the tiles can never lie about being green. The RED and AMBER counts are literally WL.filter(...) over the very same worklist array rendered just below; the Serious-AE and DLT counts are the same flags the AE&DLT section reads; the feed tile is the same boolean the freshness panel uses. There is no second computation path that could drift. If a tile says 3 open RED, there are exactly three RED rows you can click through to evidence. This is the practical payoff of the worst()-only design: the summary and the detail are the same numbers wearing different clothes.

The five tiles that carry a meaning (RED, AMBER, SAE, DLT, feeds) are clickable and jump straight to the relevant section; Enrolled and Enrollment are read-only context. A study-status pill in the header band carries the overall worst() of the hepatic, cardiac, DLT, and freshness rails — in the seeded snapshot that pill is RED, driven by the eDISH/DLT REDs and the stale feed.

The attention worklist

This is the heart of the screen: every open AMBER or RED screening flag across all checks, sorted highest-severity first, each row click-to-open into its evidence packet. It is the queue a covering biostatistician works top-down on a Monday morning. A RED row means look today and route to the medical monitor; an AMBER row means same-day review. Each row names the subject (or cohort), the check that fired, the offending values, and — crucially — the literal threshold rule that produced it, so the reader can audit the flag without leaving the row.

The checks that can land a row here are deliberately finite and deterministic:

  • eDISH / Hy’s-Law screen (RED) — the AND-gate: ALT or AST >3×ULN and total bilirubin >2×ULN, with the R-ratio and pattern shown inline. In the seeded data CP101-014 sits here.
  • QTcF / ICH E14 screen (RED >500 ms or Δ>60 ms; AMBER >480 or Δ>30) — CP101-018 (503 ms) is RED, CP101-006 (486 ms) is AMBER.
  • Serious AE recorded (RED) — reflects investigator-recorded seriousness, listing the preferred terms; never computed from severity.
  • Adjudicated DLT count vs 3+3 (RED at ≥2, AMBER at 1) — a cohort-level row. The C4 cohort (2/6) lands a RED here; this row reports that the count satisfies a trigger, it does not escalate, expand, or declare MTD.
  • Stale feed (RED) — the green-on-stale escalation, described in the next panel.

A worklist row is a screening prompt for a human — never a determination, diagnosis, adjudication, or reportable number. The eDISH row says “potential Hy’s-Law quadrant / AND-gate position pending confirmation” — it is not a Hy’s-Law case, and the rule text on the row says exactly which inequality fired. Confirmation requires a repeat draw, R-ratio / ALP cholestasis exclusion, and ruling out viral, biliary, alcohol, concomitant-drug, and underlying causes before Hy’s-Law logic is even entertained. The medical monitor, SRC, and pharmacovigilance decide; the worklist only points. See Governance for the full approved-language and decision-authority model.

Why the worklist is action-tier only — and why the silence is the point

The worklist contains only AMBER and RED. The watch tier is excluded by design, and so are the subjects who never cross any single action threshold. This is not an oversight; it is the teaching signal. Three planted cases demonstrate it — see Teaching Signals for the full roster:

  • CP101-009 — the AND-gate near-miss. ALT runs to 4.2× but bilirubin stays at 1.6× (<2×), so the AND-gate is correctly never satisfied: GREEN on eDISH at every visit (the Temple’s-Corollary region, ALT without bilirubin). It carries a real, non-serious Grade-2 “ALT increased” AE — and it is intentionally silent here. A monitor who wants to see it goes to Hepatic; the triage queue is not the place for a flag that did not fire.
  • CP101-011 — the multivariate-only case. Every univariate value sits just below its own action threshold at every visit: QTcF 474 ms is watch, not AMBER; ALT 2.6× is below 3×; Hgb and ANC are sub-Grade-2. The deterministic backbone correctly never fires, so it is absent from this list. Recognising whether such coordinated, sub-threshold drift is worth a closer human look is a matter for the reviewer’s judgement, not an automated flag. Its absence from the worklist is honest, not a miss.
  • Operational-only subjects — CP101-003 (a missed C2D1 visit) and CP101-020 (a protocol dose change with several sub-threshold lab shifts) are data-completeness / operational events, not clinical action items, so they live in Disposition, not here.

When the worklist is empty, that is information — not reassurance. The empty state literally says so: “No open AMBER/RED screening flags. Watch-tier QTcF and the multivariate-only / operational subjects are intentionally NOT here — that silence is the point: the backbone fires only on a crossed action threshold.” A deterministic, threshold-based backbone is excellent at catching a value that crosses a pre-specified line and is, by construction, blind to a slow multi-system co-drift that never crosses any single line. The worklist tells you what the backbone caught; it cannot tell you the subject is well. That gap between “nothing crossed a line” and “the subject is well” is exactly why CP101-011 is here — to make the limit of threshold-based screening visible, and to remind the reviewer that a clean worklist is not a clean bill of health.

The data-freshness / SLA panel

This panel is the dashboard’s answer to a failure mode that is easy to overlook: a screen that is perfectly green because it is reading yesterday’s data. It is a first-class safety panel, not a footnote. It lists each upstream feed with its current age, its SLA, a ten-segment load-tick history (a quick read of recent on-time loads, with the most recent ticks greyed when a feed has gone stale), and a state chip. The five feeds in the seeded snapshot are the ADaM lab feed (adam.adlb, SLA 6 h), ADaM ECG (adam.adeg, 12 h), ADaM AE (adam.adae, 24 h), the serious-AE safety feed (safe.sae, 6 h), and the IxRS randomization feed (ixrs.rand, 24 h) — the last of which is stale at 38 h in the snapshot.

The green-on-stale-data rule. Any panel that would render GREEN while sitting on a STALE feed is forced to RED, and a dedicated RED row is pushed into the attention worklist (“Stale feed — green-on-stale → RED”). A green built on stale data is the single most dangerous state on the screen, because it invites a false “all clear.” The header band, the Data-feeds tile, the freshness panel, and the worklist all reflect the same boolean — there is exactly one stale feed in the snapshot, and it escalates the entire study status to RED even though the clinical rails would otherwise allow a lower colour.

Pipeline freshness is not data-content QC

These are two different guarantees and the panel keeps them strictly apart. Freshness answers “how recently did this feed load, and is that within SLA?” — a pipeline / load-recency property. Content QC answers “is what loaded structurally valid and conformant?” — the job of validated tooling such as Pinnacle 21, not this dashboard. A feed can be perfectly fresh and full of conformance findings, or perfectly clean but eight hours past SLA. The dashboard owns only the freshness half; it never claims to have validated content, and it never substitutes for the validated QC pipeline.

How to read it in practice. Scan the State column first. A FRESH chip with full green load-ticks means the rail beneath it can be trusted to be current. A STALE → RED chip means: do not act on that domain’s green — chase the load before you conclude anything. The age-vs-SLA columns tell you how overdue, and the load-tick strip tells you whether this is a one-off late load or a feed that has been drifting. Thresholds and SLAs shown are illustrative placeholders in the synthetic build; the live version reads them from the protocol / SAP and the ops runbook.

The study heatmap

The worklist tells you who is flagged now; the heatmap tells you when it started. It is a subject × visit grid — one row per subject, one column per nominal visit (Screen, C1D1, C1D8, C1D15, C2D1, C2D8, C3D1, EOT) — where each cell shows the worst deterministic clinical screening severity at that visit. The severity per cell is a worst() over the hepatic AND-gate position and the cardiac ICH E14 QTcF tier at that visit, so the heatmap is the temporal, per-visit view of the same flags the domain sections render.

CellMeaningHow it renders

AAMBER — same-day-review tierAMBER fill
