Reading across a row reconstructs a subject’s on-study trajectory at a glance. CP101-014 shows the canonical hepatic story: green early visits, then a cell that turns RED when the AND-gate fires (with bilirubin lagging ALT by a visit, exactly as the planted signal specifies). CP101-018 shows a b at Screen (QTcF 462 already in the watch band — ringed, so the reader knows it is baseline, not the drug) and an R at the on-treatment visit where QTcF reaches 503 ms. The distinction between b and a true on-treatment R is the difference between a pre-existing condition and a signal worth chasing — the heatmap encodes it so a monitor does not misattribute a baseline finding to the compound.

A hatched (missed) cell is never benign. A missed visit can mean missed safety labs, which means the screen has nothing to evaluate at that timepoint — that is a gap to route to a human, not an implicit “no finding.” The heatmap renders it as a visibly distinct hatched cell precisely so it cannot be mistaken for a green. CP101-003’s missed C2D1 appears here as a hatch and in the deviation log in Disposition; the two views agree because they read the same visit-presence flag.

Every cell and subject label is click-through: clicking opens the subject’s evidence packet (deterministic flags first, then per-visit sparkline trajectories, then provenance) in the Subjects view, so the heatmap is also a navigation surface, not just a picture.

The backward-compatibility invariant, made visible here. The heatmap and trajectory views are per-visit, but they cannot contradict the headline plots, because the build enforces a hard rule: every per-visit series’ worst on-treatment value equals the original summary value (seed 20260611, mulberry32, reproducible). So the visit where a subject’s heatmap row turns RED is the same visit whose value drives that subject’s point on the eDISH scatter or the QTcF census. The Overview never tells a different story than the section it summarizes — it just tells the same story sooner, and shows you when it began.

A closing reminder that applies to this whole screen: every number on the Overview is a screening roll-up on synthetic, illustrative data — a prompt for a human, never a reportable number. Incidence tables, the ICH E14 categorical table, central-read intervals, MTD, and final disposition come only from validated tools (Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline). The Overview’s job is to make the validated backbone visible and to get the right human looking at the right row first.

Hepatic / DILI

The hepatic section is the clinical heart of the TRIALMON dashboard, and it is the one place where a single, deceptively simple two-axis picture — peak ALT against peak total bilirubin — carries more regulatory weight than any other view in early-phase safety monitoring. This is the home of the eDISH plot, the Hy’s-Law screening logic, the per-visit history trails, the LFT trajectories, the R-ratio pattern strip, and the peak/baseline/trend table. Everything here is a screening prompt for a human: the dashboard draws the position, fires the AND-gate, and stops. The medical monitor, pharmacovigilance, and the SRC decide what it means.

The rule that governs this entire section. No subject, point, region, or quadrant on this dashboard is ever a “Hy’s-Law case.” The only approved language is potential Hy’s-Law quadrant, Hy’s-Law screening position, or AND-gate position pending confirmation. A true Hy’s-Law determination requires confirmatory repeat testing, R-ratio / ALP cholestasis exclusion, and the affirmative ruling-out of viral hepatitis, biliary obstruction, alcohol, concomitant hepatotoxins, and underlying liver disease — a medical adjudication this dashboard cannot and does not perform. The plot points; the human decides.

The eDISH construct — what the scatter actually is

eDISH (evaluation of Drug-Induced Serious Hepatotoxicity), the diagnostic-plot convention popularized by Watkins and Senior and now embedded in FDA premarketing DILI thinking, plots two peak on-treatment values per subject: peak ALT (or AST) in multiples of ULN on the x-axis against peak total bilirubin in multiples of ULN on the y-axis. The dashboard renders the classic version: a log-scaled x-axis (because aminotransferase elevations span orders of magnitude and a linear axis would crush the informative low range), a linear-ish bilirubin axis, and two reference lines — a vertical line at ALT 3×ULN and a horizontal line at TBili 2×ULN. Those two lines carve the plane into four quadrants.

QuadrantPositionScreening meaning on this dashboard
Lower-leftALT <3×, TBili <2×Normal range / no aminotransferase or bilirubin signal. Most subjects live here.
Lower-rightALT ≥3×, TBili <2×Hepatocellular injury without hyperbilirubinemia — Temple’s Corollary region. ALT moves, bilirubin does not. This is where CP101-009 correctly stays GREEN.
Upper-leftALT <3×, TBili ≥2×Isolated hyperbilirubinemia — think Gilbert’s, hemolysis, or cholestasis. Not a hepatocellular Hy’s-Law pattern; route to a human for a different differential.
Upper-rightALT ≥3× and TBili ≥2×The potential Hy’s-Law quadrant. The AND-gate fires here. This is a screening position pending confirmation — never a case.
Why both axes, and why the AND. Hy’s original observation was that drug-induced hepatocellular injury severe enough to also raise bilirubin — i.e. enough hepatocyte loss that the liver can no longer clear bilirubin, in the absence of cholestasis or obstruction — predicts roughly a 10–50% risk of fatal or transplant-level outcomes. Neither axis alone carries that weight: ALT elevations are common and often benign (the liver adapts), and bilirubin elevations alone have many non-hepatocellular causes. The danger lives in the conjunction. That is why the screening logic is an AND-gate, not an OR.

The Hy’s-Law AND-gate as a screening position

The deterministic rule the %tm_chk_hyslaw backbone implements, and that this dashboard makes visible, is:

(ALT > 3×ULN OR AST > 3×ULN) AND (TBili > 2×ULN) → eDISH AND-gate position

with the additional epidemiological qualifier that a genuine Hy’s pattern also requires ALP not markedly elevated (to exclude a cholestatic explanation for the bilirubin) — which is why the R-ratio strip below sits immediately adjacent. The dashboard evaluates this per visit on the longitudinal data, so it can tell you not just that a subject is in the quadrant but at which visit they crossed in. When the gate fires, the cell, the eDISH point, and the worklist row turn RED, and a one-page evidence packet is what a covering biostatistician would receive.

What the gate does NOT do. Firing the AND-gate is not a diagnosis, a causality assessment, or a reportable count. It does not de-challenge, re-challenge, or adjudicate. It does not exclude Gilbert’s syndrome, hemolysis, sepsis, or a co-administered hepatotoxin — those exclusions are the medical monitor’s work. The gate’s entire job is to guarantee that a hepatocellular-plus-bilirubin position is seen by a human fast, with the lab trajectory and provenance attached. See AE & DLT for how the recorded serious AE and DLT adjudication — which the dashboard only displays, never derives — sit alongside this flag.

Per-visit HISTORY TRAILS — a record, not a prediction

The signature enhancement over a static eDISH dot is the history trail: for each subject, the dashboard connects their per-visit (ALT, TBili) positions in visit order, so you see the path a subject took through the plane to arrive at their peak. CP101-014 does not simply appear in the upper-right quadrant — you watch it travel: ALT climbs first, the point drifts rightward along the floor of the plot, and then, a visit later, bilirubin lifts it up into the quadrant. That visual lag is itself clinically informative and is the planted teaching signal (see Planted signals).

A trail is a record of where a subject HAS BEEN. It is not a forecast of where they are going. The dashboard deliberately avoids all directional and velocity language — no “trending toward,” no “trajectory pointing to,” no “rate of rise,” no extrapolation arrows. Those are inferences about the future; this deterministic backbone reports observed past values only and leaves any forward clinical judgement to the medical monitor.

The reason for that discipline is not pedantry. The moment a monitoring tool says “rising” or “heading into the quadrant,” it has made a clinical prediction, and a screening tool that predicts invites the reader to act on the prediction rather than on confirmed values. Liver enzymes plateau, recover, and reverse for reasons the dashboard cannot see. So the trail draws the dots and the connecting segments and lets the human read the shape.

LFT TRAJECTORIES — the question the static dot cannot answer

A single eDISH point answers “how high did this subject get?” It cannot answer “and then what?” The per-visit LFT trajectory small-multiples — ALT, AST, total bilirubin, ALP, and GGT each plotted against visit — answer the second question, which for DILI is often the more important one. The shape of the curve distinguishes three situations the peak value alone conflates:

  • Rising — the value is still climbing at the last observation. This is the configuration that most urgently warrants confirmatory repeat testing and closer monitoring, because the peak in the eDISH plot may not yet be the true peak.
  • Plateaued — elevated but stable across recent visits. Adaptation versus ongoing injury is a clinical judgment, but a flat high plateau reads very differently from a still-rising curve.
  • Recovering — declining from a prior peak. This is the visual substrate of what a clinician would weigh in a de-challenge assessment — but the dashboard shows the decline; it does not assert de-challenge (see the trend caveat below).
This is the operational reason the backbone went longitudinal. The hard backward-compat invariant — every per-visit series’ worst on-treatment value equals the original headline summary — means the trajectories can never contradict the eDISH peak you see in the scatter. The small-multiples add the time dimension underneath the same numbers, so a monitor gets both “how high” (the dot) and “what shape” (the curve) without the two views ever disagreeing.

The FDA premarketing DILI guidance frames LFT monitoring in escalating tiers — a routine schedule of ALT/AST/TBili/ALP, with intensified, more frequent retesting and a defined work-up once thresholds (commonly 3×, 5×, 8×ULN with concurrent bilirubin or symptoms) are crossed, and pre-specified discontinuation criteria. The trajectory panels are the dashboard’s visual analogue of that tiered thinking: they make it obvious which subjects have entered the “retest more often and start excluding alternatives” tier, without the dashboard presuming to declare the tier or trigger the protocol action itself.

The R-RATIO pattern strip

The R-ratio (sometimes the “R value”) is the CIOMS/Aithal-convention discriminator for the pattern of liver injury, computed as the ratio of the aminotransferase elevation to the alkaline-phosphatase elevation, each expressed over its own ULN:

R = (ALT / ULNALT) ÷ (ALP / ULNALP)

R valuePatternInterpretation
R ≥ 5HepatocellularAminotransferase-dominant injury — the pattern most relevant to Hy’s-Law reasoning.
2 < R < 5MixedBoth aminotransferase and cholestatic features present.
R ≤ 2CholestaticALP-dominant — raises bilirubin by a cholestatic/obstructive mechanism, which is the differential that can mimic, and must be excluded from, a Hy’s pattern.
The near-ULN instability guard. R is a ratio of two ratios, so when ALT and ALP both sit close to their own ULN, tiny absolute movements produce wild swings in R — an artifact, not a signal. The strip therefore carries an explicit “unstable near ULN” guard: when the inputs are near baseline the computed pattern is flagged as unreliable rather than reported as a clean hepatocellular/mixed/cholestatic call. A confident pattern label requires meaningfully elevated inputs.
014’s R≈4.0 (MIXED) is NOT reassurance. A reader might be tempted to treat “mixed, not pure hepatocellular” as downgrading the concern. It does not. CP101-014 still fires the eDISH AND-gate RED (ALT 5.6×, TBili 3.1×), and the R-ratio is a pattern descriptor that informs the differential — it is not a gate and it does not override the AND-gate. The R-ratio tells the medical monitor what kind of work-up to prioritize; it does not lower the priority. The screening RED stands on the AND-gate alone.

Temple’s Corollary — ALT without bilirubin

Temple’s Corollary to Hy’s Law is the observation that aminotransferase elevations without accompanying hyperbilirubinemia — even substantial, frequent ALT elevations — do not carry the same grave prognosis, because the liver frequently adapts. This is the principled basis for the AND-gate being an AND: an ALT signal in the lower-right quadrant is real and worth watching, but it is a different (and far less ominous) thing than the upper-right conjunction.

This is exactly why CP101-009 is correctly GREEN at every visit. CP101-009 reaches ALT 4.2×ULN — clearly above the 3× vertical line — but its total bilirubin never reaches 2× (it tops out at 1.6×). On the eDISH plane it lives in the lower-right Temple’s-Corollary quadrant at every visit, so the AND-gate never fires and the subject is intentionally silent in the attention worklist. That silence is a feature: it demonstrates that a high ALT alone, correctly, does not generate a Hy’s-Law screening RED. CP101-009 still carries a recorded non-serious Grade-2 “ALT increased” AE, which the monitor sees in the subject record — but it is not a quadrant flag.

The centerpiece contrast: 014 versus 009

Read side by side, these two subjects are the entire teaching argument of the section:

CP101-014CP101-009
Peak ALT5.6×ULN4.2×ULN
Peak TBili3.1×ULN (lags ALT by one visit)1.6×ULN (never reaches 2×)
eDISH quadrantUpper-right — potential Hy’s-Law quadrantLower-right — Temple’s Corollary
AND-gateFires RED at the crossing visitCorrectly GREEN every visit
R-ratio≈4.0, MIXED (does not reassure)Hepatocellular pattern; still GREEN on the gate
Recorded events (displayed, not derived)Adjudicated DLT; serious related hepatobiliary AEs; discontinued (AE)Non-serious Grade-2 “ALT increased” AE
WorklistHighest-severity open REDIntentionally silent

The lesson is that the same x-axis height (both above 3×) produces opposite screening outcomes solely because of the y-axis. 014 rises into the quadrant; the bilirubin lag means its history trail visibly travels right-then-up across visits. 009 has a higher-than-threshold ALT but a bilirubin that never crosses the line, so it stays in the benign Temple region. A dashboard that flagged on ALT alone would over-alert on 009 and bury the genuinely serious 014; the AND-gate is what keeps the signal clean.

The peak / baseline / time-to-peak / trend table

Beneath the graphics sits a per-subject table giving, for each LFT analyte, the baseline value, the worst on-treatment peak (in ×ULN), the time-to-peak (which visit), and a trend tag (the observed shape of the recent curve — rising / plateaued / recovering). Baseline matters because a value that was already elevated at screening is a different finding from one that climbed on treatment; time-to-peak matters because onset relative to first dose is part of how a monitor reasons about drug-relatedness; and the trend tag is the compact version of the trajectory shape.

“Trend” here is a description of observed values — it is NOT a de-challenge assessment. A “recovering” tag means the last few measured points are lower than the peak. It does not assert that stopping the drug caused the improvement, does not establish causality, and is not a positive de-challenge. De-challenge and re-challenge are medical determinations the investigator and monitor record; the dashboard only shows the curve they would look at. Conflating “the numbers came down” with “the drug was the cause and removing it fixed it” is precisely the inferential leap the backbone refuses to make.
How to read this section in practice. Start at the eDISH scatter to see who is in or near the upper-right quadrant. For any subject of interest, follow the history trail to see the path and the ALT/bilirubin lag, then drop to the LFT trajectory small-multiples to read rising-vs-plateaued-vs-recovering, glance at the R-ratio strip for the injury pattern (respecting the near-ULN guard), and confirm onset and baseline in the peak/baseline table. Then — for any RED — open the subject’s evidence packet, which assembles the deterministic flags, the sparkline trajectories, and provenance into the one-page hand-off a covering biostatistician would route to the medical monitor. Remember throughout: everything you are reading is a screening position. The reportable hepatic numbers come only from the validated pipeline, never from this view.

Grounding references woven through this section: FDA Guidance for Industry: Drug-Induced Liver Injury — Premarketing Clinical Evaluation (LFT monitoring tiers and discontinuation thresholds); Hy’s Law and the eDISH diagnostic plot (Zimmerman; Watkins; Senior); and the R-ratio injury-pattern convention (CIOMS; Aithal et al.). Data shown is synthetic, seeded, and illustrative — no PHI, no real subjects. See Governance for the full rule set and Planted signals for the design rationale behind 014, 009, and the other honest-by-design cases.

Cardiac / ECG

The cardiac section answers one deterministic question per subject per visit: where does this QTcF value sit relative to the pre-specified ICH E14 screening bands, and did the crossing happen on treatment? It is built around the per-visit QTcF trajectory with a cohort-median band, a categorical outlier census, and a strict separation between a screening prompt and the validated cardiac analysis. Nothing here is a central read, nothing here is a concentration–QTc estimate, and nothing here declares a cardiac safety signal. Those are the cardiologist’s and the validated analysis’s to make.

Hard rule — this is a screening census, NOT the ICH E14 table. Every count, band, and outlier label on this section is a deterministic prompt computed from seeded synthetic per-visit values. The regulated ICH E14 categorical outlier table, the central-read intervals, and any concentration–QTc (C-QTc) exposure-response analysis come ONLY from the validated analysis and the central cardiologist over-read — never from this dashboard. The census tile is labelled in-product “SCREENING CENSUS — NOT THE ICH E14 TABLE” for exactly this reason.

Why QTcF (Fridericia) is the pre-specified correction

The raw QT interval shortens as heart rate rises, so a rate correction is mandatory before any threshold has meaning. The two historically common corrections behave very differently at the heart rates you actually see in an early-phase healthy-volunteer or patient cohort:

  • Bazett (QTcB = QT/√RR) over-corrects at high heart rate and under-corrects at low heart rate. In a stimulated or anxious Phase 1 subject sitting at 90–100 bpm, Bazett can manufacture an apparent QTc prolongation that is purely an artifact of the correction formula — a false positive that wastes a monitor’s attention and can spuriously trip a stopping discussion.
  • Fridericia (QTcF = QT/RR1/3) is far flatter across the physiologic heart-rate range and is the pre-specified primary correction for CP-101. Because it is the protocol-specified correction, it is the only correction the deterministic backbone bands against. QTcB may exist in the source ECG record, but it is not what the screening logic reads.
Key concept — the correction is part of the case definition. A “QTc >500 ms” statement is meaningless without naming the correction. Mixing QTcB thresholds with QTcF values (or vice-versa) is one of the most common ways a cardiac screening pipeline generates phantom flags. The dashboard hard-codes Fridericia so that the band a subject lands in is always comparable across visits and across subjects. If you ever see a value here that disagrees with a study-lab printout, check the correction first — nine times out of ten that is the discrepancy.

The ICH E14 tier thresholds the backbone screens against

The deterministic logic uses the standard ICH E14 categorical thresholds — both the absolute QTcF value and the change-from-baseline (ΔQTcF). Two subjects with the same absolute value can carry very different meaning depending on where they started, which is why E14 has always treated absolute and delta as complementary, not redundant.

Screening tierAbsolute QTcFΔQTcF (change from own baseline)What the tile is telling a human
(below watch)≤450 ms≤30 msNo screening prompt. Silence is correct.
WATCH>450 ms—Worth a glance; NOT a worklist item on its own. Often a baseline characteristic.
AMBER>480 ms>30 msOpen prompt — route to the medical monitor.
RED>500 ms>60 msHighest-severity prompt — the value that gets a person on the phone.

The 500 ms and Δ60 ms lines are the classic E14 “values of particular concern” markers; 480 ms and Δ30 ms are the intermediate categorical bins E14 asks sponsors to tabulate. The backbone takes the worse of the absolute-band and the delta-band when it assigns a per-visit tier, so a subject who is only at 470 ms absolute — which on its own is just the watch band — but who has jumped >60 ms from a low baseline still surfaces, on the delta arm.

Honest caveat — bands are bins, not biology. A subject at 501 ms and a subject at 480 ms are one millisecond and one heartbeat of measurement noise apart, but they land in different tiers because categorical bands have hard edges. The tier is a routing decision, not a clinical verdict. Single-timepoint QTcF on a synthetic value carries the same instability as any single ECG: it should be confirmed on repeat tracings before anyone reasons about a drug effect. The dashboard never confirms; it only prompts.

The per-visit QTcF trajectory and the cohort-median band

The trajectory panel plots each subject’s QTcF across the eight nominal visits (Screen → C1D1 → C1D8 → C1D15 → C2D1 → C2D8 → C3D1 → EOT), overlaid on a shaded cohort-median band. This is the panel that lets you distinguish the two patterns that matter most for drug-relatedness reasoning:

  • A transient spike — one visit pokes above a threshold and the next returns to baseline. Often an isolated tracing, a transcription quirk, a transient autonomic event, or a single bad lead placement. It still gets a prompt, but the shape argues for “confirm before you worry.”
  • A sustained rise — the subject separates from the cohort median and stays separated, especially as dose escalates. This is the shape that genuinely concerns a cardiologist, because it tracks with exposure rather than with measurement noise.

The cohort-median band matters because an individual value is only interpretable against its peers at the same dose and visit. A subject sitting at the top of a band that has itself drifted upward cohort-wide reads very differently from a lone subject pulling away from a flat cohort. The band is a deterministic median of the cohort’s per-visit values — it is descriptive context, not a model and not a prediction.

Baseline-watch — the crossing must be on treatment

This is the single most important interpretive rule in the cardiac section, and it is why the trajectory and the census both render baseline-watch points distinctly (a separate marker style, not a flag color). A subject who is already above 450 ms at the Screen/baseline visit cannot have that value attributed to the study drug — the drug had not been given yet. A pre-existing prolonged QTc is a subject characteristic, possibly an enrollment consideration, but it is categorically NOT a treatment-emergent cardiac signal.

SubjectBaseline QTcFOn-treatment peakScreening tierWhy it reads this way
CP101-018462 ms (watch at baseline)503 msREDStarted in watch-band, but the >500 ms crossing is on treatment — that delta is the prompt, not the absolute alone.
CP101-006453 ms (watch at baseline)486 msAMBERAlready >450 at baseline (watch, not drug effect); the on-treatment rise into the 480s is what opens the AMBER prompt.

For both subjects the baseline value is shown as a watch-marker so that whoever opens the prompt immediately sees the starting point. The deterministic logic does not subtract the baseline silently and hide it — it shows both the absolute band and the on-treatment delta, so a human can see that, for example, CP101-018’s 503 ms is a real on-treatment crossing rather than a subject who merely walked in already prolonged. Contrast this with a hypothetical subject sitting at 455 ms at every visit including baseline: that is flat watch-band with no on-treatment movement, and the correct behavior is a quiet watch marker and no worklist entry.

How to read a baseline-watch subject in practice. Open the trajectory first, not the census. Ask: (1) Where did they start? (2) Is the threshold crossing on a post-dose visit? (3) Is it a single spike or a sustained separation from the cohort median? (4) Is there a plausible ΔQTcF, not just an absolute? Only after those four does the prompt mean “drug-relatedness is on the table” — and even then the answer is “get a confirmatory ECG and a cardiologist,” never a determination from this view.

The categorical outlier census

The census is a compact count panel: by cohort, how many subjects ever cross QTcF >450 / >480 / >500 ms, and how many ever show ΔQTcF >30 / >60 ms. It mirrors the shape of the regulated E14 categorical outlier table on purpose — that familiarity is what makes it useful at a glance — and that is precisely why it carries the loudest disclaimer on the page.

The census is a mirror, not the table. Because it looks like the E14 outlier table, it is the panel most likely to be mistaken for a reportable number. It is not. The counts are subject-incidence over synthetic per-visit screening values; they include baseline-watch crossings flagged as such; they have not been through central-read adjudication, manual over-read of borderline tracings, or the validated derivation. Do not paste a census count into a slide, a DSMB pack, or a regulatory document. The validated E14 table and C-QTc analysis are the only citable cardiac deliverables.

Read positively, the census earns its place: it lets a monitor see in one tile whether QTcF crossings are concentrating in the high-dose cohorts (a dose-related pattern worth a human look) or scattered as one-off baseline-watch artifacts (reassuring). It is a where-to-look tool, the cardiac analogue of the Overview attention worklist. Like every count panel in this dashboard, small-N cohorts (N=3–6) make every count statistically unstable — one subject is 17–33% of a cohort — so the census is read as “does this deserve eyes,” never as a rate.

What the cardiac section deliberately does NOT do

  • It does not compute or display concentration–QTc (C-QTc). Exposure-response is the heart of a modern E14 / E14 Q&A thorough-QT strategy and it requires paired PK and the validated analysis — see Validated & Production Tools.
  • It does not produce central-read intervals or adjudicate borderline tracings. The on-screen QTcF is a screening value, not a cardiologist over-read.
  • It does not declare a cardiac safety signal, a stopping decision, or an exposure threshold. Those belong to the medical monitor and the SRC — the same governance that runs through every section (Governance & Rules).
  • The reasoning that several sub-threshold drifts co-occur — e.g. CP101-011, whose QTcF sits at 474 ms (WATCH, not AMBER) while its other domains also stay sub-threshold — is a multi-system gestalt that no single univariate rule encodes. The deterministic backbone correctly stays silent on CP101-011 in this section, and that silence is intentional; weighing such a pattern is the human reviewer’s call. See Planted Signals.

Grounding: ICH E14 (Clinical Evaluation of QT/QTc Interval Prolongation and Proarrhythmic Potential) and the ICH E14 Q&A documents define the categorical outlier framework (absolute & change thresholds), the “values of particular concern” (>500 ms, Δ>60 ms), and the modern concentration–QTc exposure-response approach. Those documents define what a validated cardiac analysis must do; this dashboard is the screening prompt that precedes it.

Labs & Vitals

The labs & vitals section is where the dashboard is most easily misread, because it borrows the vocabulary of CTCAE grading and renal function without producing any of the regulated artifacts those words usually imply. Read it correctly and it is a fast, honest “what shifted, and when” view across hematology, hepatic-adjacent chemistry, renal function, and vital signs. Read it carelessly and a deterministic screening band on a single synthetic value gets mistaken for an adjudicated adverse-event grade. This section exists to make that mistake impossible.

Hard rule — a screening BAND is not an AE GRADE. Every “Grade” label in this section is a deterministic CTCAE-style screening band applied to a single synthetic lab value by a fixed cutoff. It is NOT an adjudicated CTCAE adverse-event grade, NOT a reportable lab abnormality, and NOT an AE. An adjudicated grade requires clinical context, repeat confirmation, attribution, and a human assessment of whether the abnormality is even an adverse event. The band tells you “this value is in the range a Grade-X cutoff would describe” — the grade tells you “a clinician evaluated this and recorded it.” The dashboard only ever does the first.

The lab-shift grid

The centerpiece is a baseline→worst lab-shift grid: for each analyte and subject, the band of the baseline value alongside the band of the worst on-treatment value, so the movement is visible, not just the endpoint. A subject who walks in at Grade-1 and worsens to Grade-3 is a very different story from one who was Grade-2 at baseline and stayed there — the shift grid makes that contrast the primary visual, exactly as a shift table does in a CSR, but as a screening prompt rather than a reportable table.

The grid uses CTCAE-style bands as the binning vocabulary because that vocabulary is what a medical monitor already thinks in. But three deliberate constraints keep it honest:

  • The band is computed from a single value — the worst on-treatment observation — not from a clinical course. CTCAE grading in real life often depends on clinical consequences (transfusion, intervention, life-threatening status) that a lone number cannot capture.
  • The grid is a screening prompt, deduplicated against, never merged into, the AE record. See the dedup note below.
  • One analyte is capped on purpose — hemoglobin — for a reason that is itself a teaching point.
Why hemoglobin is capped at Grade-3 in the value-band. CTCAE Grade-4 anemia is defined by life-threatening consequences / urgent intervention indicated — a clinical state, not a hemoglobin number. There is no Hgb value that, by itself, “is” Grade-4; that determination needs a monitor looking at the whole picture. So the deterministic value-band tops out at Grade-3. A subject whose Hgb falls into the severe range is banded Grade-3 and routed to a human; the dashboard refuses to assert Grade-4 because it cannot, by construction, know whether the clinical Grade-4 criteria are met. This is the single most important example of the band-vs-grade distinction made concrete.

Hematology trajectories — shape is the signal

For hemoglobin and ANC the section adds per-visit trajectory panels, because with cytopenias the shape of the curve carries more information than any single worst value:

  • Dip-and-recover — a nadir at one visit that rebounds by the next. Often a transient, self-limited effect; the worst value still bands and prompts, but the recovery is reassuring context a single number would hide.
  • Progressive decline — each visit lower than the last, no recovery. This is the shape that concerns a monitor: it can signal cumulative marrow suppression or ongoing hemolysis and it tends to track with continued or escalating dosing.

Two subjects can share an identical “worst Hgb” band and need completely different responses — one is recovering, one is still falling. The lab-shift grid alone would flatten that distinction; the trajectory restores it. The same logic applies to ANC for neutropenia. As always, the trajectory is a record of where the subject has been, not a forecast of where they are going.

The renal panel

The renal panel deliberately leads with creatinine fold-rise over the subject’s OWN baseline rather than with an absolute creatinine or a single reference range. This is the most clinically important design choice in the section:

Why fold-rise over own baseline catches what an in-range value hides. A young, muscular, or simply low-baseline subject can double their creatinine — a clear acute-kidney-injury (AKI) staging signal — while the absolute value is still inside the population reference range. Screen against the reference range alone and you miss the early AKI entirely. Screen against the subject’s own baseline (the KDIGO-style 1.5× / 2× / 3× fold-rise logic) and the rise surfaces immediately, exactly when a monitor can still act. The panel is built around this relative view on purpose.

Alongside the fold-rise the panel shows an eGFR estimate using CKD-EPI 2021 (race-free). Two firm caveats travel with it:

  • It is an ESTIMATE and a screening context value — never a reported renal endpoint. The race-free 2021 equation is the current standard for estimation, but eGFR is an approximation of GFR, not a measurement of it, and it is least reliable exactly where creatinine is changing fastest (non–steady-state AKI).
  • Any renal value that appears in a regulated document — a CSR renal narrative, a safety table, a DSMB renal summary — comes from the validated pipeline, not from this estimate. See Validated & Production Tools.

Vitals & orthostatic PCS — operational context, never a tier

The vitals board shows blood pressure, heart rate, and an orthostatic / potentially-clinically-significant (PCS) view — supine-to-standing changes that flag possible orthostatic hypotension. This board is deliberately the only domain in the section that does not assign a severity tier.

Why vitals carry no tier. A PCS vitals reading is enormously context-dependent — posture, timing relative to dose, recent activity, hydration, anxiety, cuff size, a missed rest period. Banding it into RED/AMBER would imply a determination the value cannot support and would flood the worklist with operational noise. So the vitals board is presented as operational context that sits next to the clinical domains and informs how a monitor reads them, but never as a tier and never as a worklist-driving flag on its own. It answers “what was this subject’s hemodynamic state around the visit,” not “is this an abnormality of severity X.”

This mirrors the discipline used elsewhere: operational and contextual information is shown distinctly from clinical screening tiers, the same way missed visits are operational on the Overview heatmap and the way Disposition separates RBQM operational KRIs from clinical flags. CP101-020 — the protocol dose change where several labs shift at once but each stays sub-threshold — is a good case to read here: the vitals and lab context together explain a coordinated, operationally-driven drift that no single band would (or should) escalate.

The lab grid and the AE panel are independent sources — deduplicate them

Two doors to the same room. The lab-shift grid (this section) and the AE incidence matrix in AE & DLT are independent sources. A lab abnormality may appear as a banded shift here AND as a recorded AE there — or it may appear in only one. The two are NOT the same record and must not be summed. The monitor’s job is to deduplicate: confirm whether a banded lab shift corresponds to an already-recorded AE, a not-yet-recorded one, or a clinically-not-significant value that needs no AE at all. The grid is a screening prompt to check the AE record, not a second AE count. Treating it as additive double-counts and inflates apparent incidence.

This independence is a feature, not a redundancy. The lab grid catches abnormalities that may not yet be entered as AEs (the value is in the data before anyone has assessed it); the AE record carries the clinical assessment, attribution, and seriousness that the lab value alone never has. Reading them against each other — band here, recorded AE there, or a gap between them — is one of the highest-yield reconciliation tasks in safety review, and it is exactly the workflow this layout is meant to provoke.

What this section does NOT produce

  • No adjudicated CTCAE grades — only deterministic value-bands using CTCAE-style cutoffs as vocabulary.
  • No reportable lab abnormality counts or shift tables — those are validated-pipeline deliverables.
  • No reported renal endpoints — the eGFR is a CKD-EPI-2021 estimate for screening context only.
  • No severity tier on vitals — vitals are operational context by design.
  • No seriousness or causality — those are investigator/SRC-recorded assessments the dashboard would only ever display, never derive (see Governance & Rules).

Grounding: CTCAE provides the grading vocabulary the bands borrow (and the Grade-4 anemia definition that justifies the Hgb cap); KDIGO provides the fold-rise-over-baseline logic for early AKI; CKD-EPI 2021 is the current race-free eGFR estimating equation. All three describe what a validated lab-safety analysis must do — this dashboard is the screening prompt that points a human toward it, on synthetic data, with no PHI and no reportable output.

AE & DLT

This section is where adverse events, dose-limiting toxicities, and the 3+3 escalation rhythm of CP-101 are made visible. It carries three panels — an AE incidence matrix (SOC × CTCAE grade), a 3+3 escalation state ladder, and an AE onset/duration swimlane — and it is the part of the dashboard most likely to be mistaken for a decision engine. It is not one. Every panel here displays recorded or auditable values and computes which 3+3 branch a DLT count satisfies; it never escalates, expands, de-escalates, or declares an MTD. The Safety Review Committee does that.

The one rule that governs this whole section. The dashboard NEVER makes the escalation decision. It reads the planned cohort size from the protocol design, displays the SRC-adjudicated DLT count, and labels which 3+3 branch that count satisfies (for example “2 of 6 → MTD-exceeded TRIGGER”). That label is a prompt to convene, not an action. Escalation, expansion, de-escalation, MTD declaration, and dose-decision are SRC determinations recorded outside this tool. A screenshot of this ladder is not a dose decision and must never be filed as one.

What this section derives versus what it only displays

Keeping this boundary crisp is the difference between a teaching dashboard and an unvalidated clinical decision system. The split is deliberate and narrow.

QuantityOriginDashboard’s role
TEAE flag (treatment-emergent: onset on/after first dose)Deterministic rule on recorded onset date vs first-dose dateDerives by auditable rule
3+3 state label (trigger / decision-pending branch)Deterministic rule on planned N + adjudicated DLT countDerives by auditable rule
DLT (yes/no, which event)SRC adjudication against protocol DLT definitionDisplays only
Causality / relationship to study drugInvestigator-recorded assessmentDisplays only
Seriousness (ICH E2A criteria)Investigator/PV-recorded assessmentDisplays only
CTCAE grade on a reported AEInvestigator-assigned per CTCAE v5Displays only
Reported AE incidence table, MTD, RP2DValidated SDTM/ADaM pipeline + SRCNever produced here
A note on the word “grade.” Elsewhere in this dashboard — in the Labs & Vitals shift grid, for instance — a “CTCAE band” is a deterministic screening band placed on a single lab value, not an adjudicated AE grade. In this section the grade shown in the AE matrix is the investigator-assigned CTCAE v5 grade on a reported adverse event. Same vocabulary, two different objects. The matrix shows recorded AE grades; the lab grid shows screening bands. Do not pool them.

The AE incidence matrix (SOC × CTCAE grade)

The matrix is a subject-incidence table: each cell counts subjects (not events) who experienced at least one AE in that System Organ Class at that worst CTCAE grade. Counting is worst-grade-per-subject-per-SOC — a subject with three Grade-1 headaches and one Grade-3 headache contributes once, to the Grade-3 Nervous-system cell. This mirrors the convention of the validated incidence table without being it.

  • Rows are MedDRA-style System Organ Classes (controlled vocabulary only — no verbatim terms, no narrative prose).
  • Columns are CTCAE v5 grades 1–5, plus a row/column total.
  • Cells are de-duplicated subject counts at worst grade.
Grade-5 is structurally zero. This is synthetic illustrative data with no death and no Grade-5 event anywhere. The Grade-5 column exists so the reader can see that it is empty — the absence is part of the teaching, and it keeps the matrix shape honest against a real CTCAE table. If you ever see a non-zero Grade-5 cell here, the data has been tampered with.

Severity and seriousness are different axes — with both counter-examples

This is the single most common confusion in early-phase safety review, so the dashboard keeps the two concepts on visibly separate tracks and plants a counter-example in each direction.

  • SEVERITY = CTCAE v5 grade (1 mild → 5 death). It describes intensity.
  • SERIOUSNESS = the ICH E2A regulatory criteria: results in death, is life-threatening, requires/prolongs hospitalization, causes persistent or significant disability/incapacity, is a congenital anomaly, or is an other important medical event. It is a regulatory category, recorded by the investigator/PV, not computed.
Counter-example A — severe but not serious. A Grade-3 (severe) laboratory or symptomatic AE that resolves with supportive care and meets no E2A criterion is severe, not serious. The matrix will show it in a Grade-3 cell; the seriousness flag stays off. A high grade does not automatically promote an event to serious.

Counter-example B — serious but not high-grade. A Grade-2 event that nevertheless requires inpatient hospitalization is serious by E2A despite a moderate grade. The dashboard shows the serious flag on a Grade-2 row. Seriousness is not a function of grade.

Because of this, the matrix never lets a grade drive a seriousness count and never lets seriousness inflate a grade. Where seriousness is shown, it is the recorded E2A determination displayed verbatim from the data, never inferred from the grade column. The signature hepatobiliary events on CP101-014 are recorded as serious and related — both attributes are investigator/SRC-recorded and merely surfaced here.

The 3+3 escalation state ladder

The ladder is the centerpiece of this section: one rung per dose cohort, each showing planned N, the number evaluable for DLT, the SRC-adjudicated DLT count, and the 3+3 branch that count satisfies — expressed as a TRIGGER or a decision-pending label.

The classic 3+3 algorithm the ladder mirrors:

  1. Treat 3 subjects at a dose. 0/3 DLTs → the design permits escalation to the next dose (SRC decides).
  2. 1/3 DLTs → expand that cohort to 6 (SRC decides). Then 1/6 permits escalation; ≥2/6 means the dose has exceeded the maximum tolerated dose.
  3. ≥2/3 DLTs at first pass → dose exceeded; the SRC typically declares the prior dose the MTD after expanding/confirming it.
DLT window = 28 days. A subject is “evaluable for DLT” only after completing the protocol-defined DLT observation window (here 28 days) or experiencing a DLT within it. The “evaluable” count on each rung reflects this. A cohort can be fully enrolled yet not fully evaluable — the ladder shows both numbers so you never mistake enrollment for assessability.

Planned N is read from the design — C3 was designed N=6

Planned N is a protocol fact, not a dashboard inference. The ladder reads each cohort’s planned size from the study design. Cohort C3 was designed at N=6; it was not “expanded from 3.” The dashboard must never render C3 as a 1/3 → expansion event. Conflating a designed-larger cohort with an algorithmic expansion would misrepresent the trial’s decision history — exactly the kind of fabricated narrative this dashboard is built to avoid.

This matters because an external reader scanning a 3+3 ladder instinctively reads any N=6 cohort as “it must have had a DLT and expanded.” For C3 that inference is wrong: the larger size was pre-specified. CP101-010 contributes 1 DLT in C3, which under the algorithm is the “permits escalation” branch — not an MTD trigger — but again, the SRC decides.

The C4 centerpiece: 2 of 6 → MTD-exceeded TRIGGER

Cohort C4 (120 mg, N=6) carries two adjudicated DLTs — CP101-013 and CP101-014. Two of six satisfies the ≥2/6 branch, which the ladder labels MTD-EXCEEDED TRIGGER — SRC decision pending. This is the loudest thing the ladder will ever say, and it is still only a prompt.

The label reads TRIGGER, not MTD exceeded. The dashboard computes that the count satisfies the MTD-exceeded branch of the 3+3 rule. It does not declare that the MTD has been exceeded, does not nominate the prior dose as the MTD, and does not stop or de-escalate the cohort. Those are SRC actions taken in a convened review, recorded in the validated system — not here. The trigger’s job is to make sure the SRC is convened, not to pre-empt it.

Note the cross-domain coherence: CP101-014 is also the planted Hy’s-Law screening RED (ALT 5.6×, TBili 3.1×, AND-gate position) and is recorded with serious related hepatobiliary AEs, discontinued for AE. The same subject therefore appears as a hepatic screening flag, a serious-AE row in the matrix, a DLT on the C4 rung, and a discontinuation in Disposition. One subject, four faithful views — never four invented stories.

Small-N instability, stated plainly. With 3–6 subjects per cohort, a single adjudication flips a branch. “2 of 6” is 33% with a confidence interval that spans most of the unit interval. The ladder shows the count, not a rate, precisely so no one over-reads a stable-looking percentage. Every rate derived from these denominators carries the small-N caveat, and the ladder is a convening prompt — not an estimate of the true toxicity rate.

The AE onset/duration swimlane

The swimlane plots each AE as a horizontal bar against study day — onset to resolution (or ongoing) — one lane per subject or per event, with the 28-day DLT window shaded. This is the panel that lets a monitor reason about drug-relatedness through timing.

How a monitor reads it. Clustering of events shortly after a dose, and especially inside the 28-day DLT window, is the temporal pattern that raises the question of drug-relatedness — it is how monitors reason, not how they conclude. Late, isolated, or pre-dose-anchored events read differently. The swimlane shows the timing; the investigator records the relationship. Read the C4 DLTs’ bars against the shaded window to see why they were adjudicated within it.

The swimlane deliberately stays in controlled vocabulary — event term, grade band, serious flag, relationship-as-recorded, onset/resolution study day. There is no case-narrative prose, no free text, nothing identity-narrowing. Timing and recorded attributes only; the human supplies the clinical story.

What this section will not tell you

  • It will not give you the reported AE incidence table — that comes from the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline and Pinnacle 21, never from this matrix. The matrix is a screening census, not the regulatory table.
  • It will not declare an MTD or RP2D. The ladder triggers; the SRC declares.
  • It will not adjudicate a DLT, assign causality, or set seriousness. It displays those recorded assessments.
  • It will not produce a Grade-5/death — structurally absent by design.

See Planted Signals for the full roster of teaching cases (CP101-014, -013, -010, -009, -011, -018, -006), Governance for the derive-vs-display contract in full, and Hepatic for the eDISH/Hy’s-Law screening logic that feeds CP101-014’s DLT story.

Disposition & KRIs

This section is the operational backbone of CP-101 — who screened, who enrolled, who is ongoing, who left and why; the risk-based quality-management (RBQM) signals that say “look here”; and the visit-compliance grid that decides whether a missed window quietly threatens the integrity of a PK or ECG endpoint. Nothing on this page is a safety determination or a reportable number. It is the ICH E6 central-monitoring view: it surfaces operational facts and recorded reasons so a human knows where to direct attention.

Operational only — no PHI, no reportable safety numbers, no adjudication. Disposition reasons, deviation severities, and KRI bands shown here are recorded or banded, never adjudicated. The final disposition table, the audited deviation classification, and any reportable figure come from the validated pipeline and the clinical-operations system of record — not from this dashboard. This page is a navigation aid for monitoring, not a record.

The enrollment / disposition funnel

The funnel walks a subject cohort from first contact to current state: screened → screen-failed / enrolled → ongoing / completed / discontinued, with discontinuations broken out by recorded reason. For CP-101 that is 22 enrolled across cohorts C1–C5 plus the screen-failures that never entered.

  • Screened — all subjects who consented and entered screening.
  • Screen-failed — did not meet eligibility; shown with recorded fail reason category (controlled vocabulary, no narrative).
  • Enrolled — received study drug; this is the denominator that feeds the 3+3 ladder’s planned-vs-actual view.
  • Ongoing / Completed / Discontinued — current state, with discontinuations split by recorded reason (AE, withdrawal of consent, lost to follow-up, protocol-specified criterion, etc.).
Recorded reason, not adjudicated cause. “Discontinued — AE” is the reason captured in the operational record. It is not a causality assessment, not a regulatory determination, and not a statement that the AE was drug-related. The funnel displays the recorded category; the medical monitor and PV own the interpretation. The final, reportable disposition tabulation is a validated-pipeline output — this funnel is the in-flight monitoring view, which can change as data matures.

CP101-014 appears here as a discontinuation with recorded reason “AE,” coherent with its serious related hepatobiliary events and its C4 DLT in AE & DLT. The same subject, the same fact, displayed in the operational frame — not a second, invented story.

RBQM — KRI tiles

The key-risk-indicator tiles are the ICH E6(R2/R3) central-monitoring layer: a small set of operational metrics, each banded green / amber / red against a pre-specified threshold, that direct a monitor’s attention across subjects and (in a real study) sites. Typical CP-101 KRIs include screen-failure rate, AE under-reporting proxies, query rate/aging, overdue data entry, visit out-of-window rate, and protocol-deviation density.

A KRI band is a “look here” prompt, not a verdict. An amber or red tile means a metric crossed a pre-specified operational threshold and warrants human attention — it does not assert that quality is compromised, that a site is non-compliant, or that any safety conclusion follows. The band routes attention; the central monitor investigates and decides. KRIs are operational-quality signals and must never be read as clinical-safety signals — those live in the safety sections and are owned by the medical monitor.
Small denominators wobble. With 22 subjects and 3–6 per cohort, a single event can swing a rate-based KRI across a band boundary. The tiles show the count and the denominator alongside the band so a monitor can sanity-check whether a red is a real trend or one event against a tiny base. Treat band-crossings on small N as questions, not findings.

The protocol-deviation log

The deviation log is a controlled-vocabulary list of recorded protocol deviations: subject, category, recorded severity (e.g. minor / major as classified in the operational record), study day, and disposition. It is the granular companion to the deviation-density KRI — the KRI says “a lot of deviations are clustering,” the log says which ones.

  • Severity is shown as recorded, not re-graded by the dashboard. A “major” deviation is major because the operational record classifies it so; the dashboard does not adjudicate or re-classify.
  • No free-text narrative — category codes and recorded fields only, consistent with the no-PHI, no-prose constraint that runs through the whole tool.
  • Dose-change deviations are first-class because they ripple into the safety picture without being safety signals themselves.
How the dose-change case reads. CP101-020 carries a recorded dose-change protocol deviation. It is purely operational — several of its labs shift at once around the change, but every value stays sub-threshold, so the deterministic safety backbone correctly never fires a clinical flag for it. The teaching point: a deviation can move data without being a toxicity signal. The monitor sees the deviation in this log and the coherent (sub-threshold) lab wobble in Labs & Vitals, and recognizes operational cause — not a safety event. Do not let the simultaneous lab shifts get read as a safety signal; with a recorded operational cause and every value sub-threshold, this deterministic view correctly treats it as a deviation to note, not a toxicity.

The visit-compliance matrix

The matrix is subject × visit, one cell per scheduled visit, classified as in-window, out-of-window, or missed against the protocol’s 8 nominal visits (Screen, C1D1, C1D8, C1D15, C2D1, C2D8, C3D1, EOT). It is the operational twin of the Overview study heatmap: the heatmap answers “when did clinical severity start,” this answers “did the visit happen on time at all.”

Cell stateMeaningWhy it matters operationally
In-windowVisit occurred within the protocol-allowed windowEndpoint timing intact
Out-of-windowVisit occurred, but outside the allowed windowThreatens PK / ECG / NCA validity (see below)
MissedVisit did not occurPossible missing safety labs → route to a human
Out-of-window is not cosmetic — it can invalidate the science. Early-phase endpoints are timing-dependent. A PK sample drawn off-nominal corrupts the actual-time-vs-nominal-time relationship the NCA (non-compartmental analysis, computed in the validated tool — Phoenix WinNonlin, never here) depends on; an ECG read off-schedule disturbs concentration-QTc pairing. The matrix surfaces out-of-window cells so the analyst can decide whether a record needs actual-time handling, exclusion, or a protocol-deviation entry. The dashboard flags the timing; the PK scientist and statistician decide the analytic consequence.
A missed visit is never “fine” — it routes to a human. A missed visit can mean missed safety labs, which means the deterministic safety backbone has nothing to evaluate for that timepoint — an absence of a flag is not evidence of safety. The matrix shows the gap precisely so it is investigated, not silently treated as a clean record. Silence from missing data must be escalated, never interpreted as reassurance.

CP101-003 is the planted missed-visit case: an operational data-completeness gap, not a clinical event. It shows as a missed cell here and as a hatched/operational cell on the Overview heatmap — and crucially it is excluded from the safety Attention Worklist, because a missing visit is an operations question routed to a human, not a clinical screening flag. That deliberate silence in the safety worklist, paired with visibility here, is the teaching point.

How this section connects to the rest of the dashboard

  • The funnel’s enrolled count is the denominator behind the 3+3 ladder; its discontinued-AE rows (CP101-014) are coherent with that section’s DLT and serious-AE displays.
  • The missed/out-of-window cells reconcile with the Overview heatmap’s hatched operational cells and the data-freshness panel.
  • The dose-change deviation (CP101-020) explains the coherent sub-threshold lab wobble seen in Labs & Vitals.
  • Everything reportable — final disposition, audited deviation classifications, validated KRIs — comes from the validated pipeline, never from this page.

See Governance for the recorded-vs-adjudicated and screening-vs-reportable contracts in full, and Planted Signals for CP101-003 and CP101-020 in context.

Subject drill-down — the roster & the evidence packet

Every other view in this dashboard is a population lens: the worklist, the heatmap, the eDISH scatter, the 3+3 ladder all let you reason across subjects. The Subjects section is the inverse — it is where you land on one person and assemble the full deterministic story behind a flag (or behind a deliberate silence). It is two tightly coupled tools: a sortable / filterable / searchable roster that is the index of the trial, and a click-anywhere evidence-packet modal that is the screening dossier for a single subject. This is the section a covering biostatistician or medical monitor opens when someone asks “what is going on with CP101-014” — and, just as importantly, the section that lets you prove a negative: that the quiet near-misses are quiet because the backbone read them correctly, not because it missed them.

The roster — the trial’s index

The roster is a single table of all 22 subjects, one row each, with the screening-relevant headline values and a set of per-domain flag chips. The columns are deliberately the smallest set that lets you triage a row without opening it:

ColumnWhat it showsSortable
SubjectThe subject id (e.g. CP101-014) — click anywhere on the row to open the evidence packetyes
Cohort · doseCohort C1–C5 and the assigned dose level (10/30/60/120/200 mg)yes
Sex / AgeDemographics for context — never an identity-narrowing field, never a tier inputno
Peak ALTWorst on-treatment ALT ×ULN (the eDISH x-axis value)yes
Peak TBiliWorst on-treatment total bilirubin ×ULN (the eDISH y-axis value)yes
Max QTcFWorst on-treatment QTcF (ms); a (watch) annotation marks the 450–480 band that is not an action tieryes
TEAECount of treatment-emergent AEs (the one AE quantity the dashboard derives; seriousness and grade are recorded, not derived)yes
Screening flagsPer-domain chips — RED / AMBER / watch / clear — one per domain that has anything to say (eDISH, QTcF, DLT, serious AE)yes (by worst flag)

Click a column header to sort; click again to reverse. The default sort is by subject id. Sorting by Peak ALT or Max QTcF floats the most extreme values to the top; sorting by Screening flags uses a worst-flag rank (RED > AMBER > watch > ops > clear) so the action-tier subjects cluster together.

Filter and search

Above the table is a filter bar with three controls that compose (they all apply at once):

  • Search subject id — free-text, case-insensitive substring match on the id. Type 014 to jump straight to CP101-014.
  • Cohort — restrict to one of C1–C5. Useful when you are reasoning about a single dose level’s 3+3 picture and want the matching subjects in front of you (pairs naturally with AE & DLT).
  • Flag state — the triage filter: Has RED, Has AMBER, or Clear / watch only. “Has RED” matches an eDISH RED, a QTcF RED, or a recorded serious AE; “Has AMBER” matches a QTcF AMBER or an adjudicated DLT; “Clear / watch only” is the complement — everyone with no action-tier flag, including the watch-band and the multivariate-only subjects.
How to use it in a stand-up. Start with Flag state → Has RED to get the irreducible action list (this should agree, subject-for-subject, with the RED rows of the Overview worklist — they are computed from the same flags). Then switch to Clear / watch only and read it as a second list: these are the subjects the backbone is deliberately not escalating, and you should be able to say why for each one.

Confirming the near-misses are correctly silent

The roster is the natural place to verify the two planted near-misses — the cases that exist precisely to demonstrate the backbone’s discipline, that it fires on a crossed action threshold and nothing softer. (For the full catalogue of planted signals see The planted teaching signals.)

  • CP101-009 — the eDISH AND-gate near-miss. Its Peak ALT reads 4.2× (well above the 3× you might reflexively flag on) but its Peak TBili reads 1.6×, which is below the 2× bilirubin arm of the AND-gate. So its eDISH chip is clear, not RED — it sits in the Temple’s-Corollary region (ALT without bilirubin), the lower-right of the eDISH plot. It does carry a non-serious Grade-2 “ALT increased” AE, which is real and shows in the packet, but it is intentionally silent in the worklist because no action threshold is crossed. Filtering Has RED must not surface 009; filtering Clear / watch only must.
  • CP101-011 — the multivariate-only case. Every univariate value sits below its own action threshold at every visit: QTcF 474 ms is in the watch band (not AMBER), ALT 2.6× is below 3×, Hgb and ANC are sub-Grade-2. Several organs drift together, but no single rule crosses — so the deterministic backbone correctly produces only a watch chip and never an action flag. CP101-012 is a milder, more ambiguous co-drift of the same kind. The coordinated-drift question (“is this gestalt worth a closer human look even though nothing crossed?”) is left to the reviewer’s judgement — the validated backbone makes no such call, by design.
Silence is a finding here, not a gap. When you confirm 009 and 011 are not on the RED list, you are not finding a hole in coverage — you are confirming the backbone refuses to manufacture an action flag out of a sub-threshold value. That refusal is what makes the flags it does raise worth a human’s time. A monitor still chooses to look at a watch-band trend; the dashboard simply does not page on it.

The evidence packet — one subject, the whole deterministic story

Click any subject row (or any subject point/cell anywhere else in the dashboard — the eDISH scatter, the heatmap, a swimlane) and the evidence-packet modal opens. It is laid out in a strict, deliberate order — conclusions you can act on first, raw trajectories second, provenance last — so a reviewer reads the same way every time.

1. Header & demographics

The subject id, carried with a SYNTHETIC tag (and an OPERATIONAL tag for the decoy subjects like CP101-003 and CP101-020), then cohort, dose, sex, age, enrollment day, and disposition with its recorded reason. Disposition and reason are displayed as recorded — the dashboard never derives why someone discontinued.

2. Deterministic evidence (the validated backbone) — first

A compact table, one row per screening check, each with the worst/peak value and the resulting screening flag:

  • eDISH / Hy’s-Law — peak ALT, AST, TBili, ALP (all ×ULN) and the computed R-ratio with its pattern label (hepatocellular / mixed / cholestatic, or unstable when values sit near ULN). For CP101-014 this is the RED AND-gate row: ALT 5.6×, TBili 3.1×, R≈4.0 = MIXED (not hepatocellular).
  • QTcF (ICH E14 screen) — max QTcF and ΔQTcF, banded RED (>500) / AMBER (>480) / WATCH (>450, <480). The watch row is labelled as watch so no one mistakes it for an action tier.
  • Adjudicated DLT — whether the SRC recorded a candidate DLT. This is displayed, never adjudicated by the dashboard.
  • AEs (TEAE / serious / Gr≥3) — the TEAE count (derived), plus whether any AE is recorded serious or recorded Grade≥3. Severity and seriousness are shown as separate facts.

This table is the part of the packet that maps one-to-one to a flag a human must act on. Everything below it is supporting context.

3. Per-visit trajectories (as-collected, single-draw) — second

Six labelled sparklines, one per key biomarker, each a small line over the nominal visit sequence with the subject’s last/worst value called out and CTCAE-style band lines drawn behind:

  • ALT ×ULN and TBili ×ULN — the eDISH pair, baseline ringed. On CP101-014 you can read the planted lag directly: TBili rises a visit after ALT, which is exactly the temporal pattern a monitor wants to see before entertaining Hy’s-Law logic.
  • QTcF (ms) — with 450/480/500 band lines; baseline ringed so a subject who was already in the watch band at baseline (e.g. CP101-018’s 462 ms) reads as a pre-existing finding, not a drug effect.
  • Hgb (g/dL) and ANC (109/L) — hematology trajectories, shown for context, not as a tier.
  • Creatinine — the renal trajectory, again context.

Missing visits are not interpolated — a missing draw is drawn as a small marker on the baseline rather than a connected point, so a gap reads as a gap (see CP101-003). Because of the hard backward-compatibility invariant, each sparkline’s worst on-treatment point equals the headline peak shown in the deterministic table above it — the trajectory can never silently contradict the flag.

4. Provenance — last

If the subject carries a provenance note (every planted subject does), it appears at the foot of the packet in plain language: what the flag is, why it fired or deliberately did not, and any operational caveat. This is the “read me before you escalate” line — e.g. 009’s note explicitly states that TBili 1.6× keeps it off the AND-gate, and 011’s note explicitly states every value is sub-threshold, so the backbone correctly does not fire.

The packet is a screening dossier, not a determination. Everything in it — the flags, the bands, the trajectories — is a prompt for a human on synthetic data. None of it is a diagnosis, an adjudication, or a reportable number. Vitals and hematology are shown for context, never as a tier. Relationship/causality, seriousness, expectedness, and DLT status are investigator/SRC-recorded and only displayed. Reported numbers come only from the validated tools (Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline) — never this packet. The medical monitor, SRC, and pharmacovigilance decide.

Hover tooltips — triage without opening

Throughout the dashboard, hovering a subject point (for example a dot on the eDISH scatter) raises a compact tooltip: the id, cohort · dose · sex · age, the ALT/TBili pair with its eDISH state, the QTcF/ΔQTcF with any watch annotation, an adjudicated-DLT line when present, and a “click for the evidence packet” hint. The tooltip is a one-second triage — enough to decide whether a point is worth a click — and uses the same colour grammar as the rest of the dashboard (RED / AMBER / GREEN / steel-blue watch). It carries no value the packet does not.

How to use the section in practice

  1. Triage from a population view, drill from the roster. When the worklist or heatmap surfaces a subject, jump to Subjects, search the id, and open the packet to read the deterministic table and trajectories together before forming any view.
  2. Read the deterministic table before the sparklines. The flags are the actionable layer; the trajectories explain shape and timing (the TBili lag, the baseline-watch ring) but do not change the flag.
  3. Use “Clear / watch only” as an audit, not a dismissal. Walk that list and confirm you can name why each subject is sub-threshold — this is where 009, 011, and 012 live, and where the backbone’s discipline is checked.
  4. Treat a provenance note as required reading. It is the difference between “ALT 4.2×, escalate” and “ALT 4.2× but bilirubin clean, this is Temple’s Corollary, watch the repeat.”
  5. Never quote a packet number into a report. If a value needs to be reportable, it comes from the validated pipeline — the packet is where you decide to ask the human, not where the number is born.

From demo to production — how this maps to the real %tm_* job

This dashboard is the validated backbone, made visible — a teaching view of what the deterministic %tm_* monitoring job produces, rendered on seeded synthetic data so the picture is reproducible and contains no PHI. It is a viewer of screening flags, not the job, not a record, and not a source of reported numbers. The job itself is the thing that runs on a schedule against real study data: it detects threshold crossings, packages the evidence, and pages a human — and the human decides. This section is the explicit crosswalk: what stays identical when you move from this illustration to the real monitoring job, and what must change.

The one-line mental model

The dashboard is the window; the job is the engine. In production, the %tm_* job runs against validated data, applies protocol/SAP-specified rules, and emits a flag table plus an evidence package. A viewer like this one renders that flag table. The logic you see here — the AND-gate, the QTcF bands, the 3+3 state machine, the freshness escalation — is the same logic; only the inputs and the thresholds’ provenance change.

What changes

DimensionIn this demoIn the production %tm_* job
Data sourceSeeded synthetic CP-101 (SEED 20260611, mulberry32), generated in-pageReal validated SDTM/ADaM from the study’s SDTM/ADaM pipeline (LB, EG, AE, DS, EX, VS …)
ThresholdsIllustrative (3×/5× ALT, 2× TBili, 450/480/500 ms, CTCAE-style bands)Protocol- and SAP-specified action thresholds, with the pre-specified QTc correction (Fridericia) and the protocol’s DILI/cardiac stopping criteria
Freshness SLAsIllustrative per-feed ages and SLAs (the green-on-stale demo)The study’s real data-transfer SLAs and load schedule, with the same escalation rule (a green built on a stale feed → RED)
