Reported numbersNone — every count is labelled a screening census, not a tableCome only from validated tools: PK/QTc from Phoenix WinNonlin, dataset conformance from Pinnacle 21, incidence/disposition/MTD from the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline — never the job’s screening flags
DLT & 3+3DLT flags are pre-seeded; the dashboard computes the 3+3 state onlyDLT status is SRC-adjudicated and recorded upstream; the job computes the same trigger/decision-pending state and still never escalates/expands/declares MTD — the SRC does
DeliveryA static viewer you openA scheduled job that runs, detects, packages an evidence bundle, and pages the on-call monitor / covering biostatistician (heartbeat + dead-man’s-switch watchdog)
IdentitySynthetic ids, no PHI, no kit/randomization codesReal subject keys behind the study’s access controls; same rule that no identity-narrowing free text or case-narrative prose is generated

What stays exactly the same

  • The governance model. A flag is a screening prompt for a human, never a determination. Severity and seriousness stay separate. Relationship, seriousness, expectedness, and DLT status are recorded upstream and only displayed. The full set of rules in The governance model is identical in production.
  • The deterministic logic. The eDISH AND-gate (ALT≥X ×ULN and TBili≥2×ULN, with R-ratio cholestasis exclusion), the QTcF banding, the freshness escalation, the 3+3 state machine, the TEAE derivation — same auditable rules. The demo and the job are the same screening engine over different inputs.
  • The role split. The job does detect / package / page; humans decide. The dashboard views the flags. Neither ever adjudicates, diagnoses, or declares MTD.
  • The viewer/record distinction. Neither the demo nor any production viewer is a regulated record or a source of reported numbers. Count panels that mirror a regulatory table stay labelled “screening census — not the validated table.”

What must be validated as study programs

Moving from illustration to a monitoring job that touches real study data brings the screening logic into scope for risk-based computerized-system validation (CSV). Two things in particular are validated as study programs:

  1. The threshold / flag logic. The deterministic rules that turn a validated ADaM value into a screening flag — the AND-gate, the band cut-points, the R-ratio exclusion, the freshness escalation, the 3+3 state map — are specified against the protocol/SAP and tested (known-input → expected-flag cases, including the boundary and near-miss cases this demo plants). The flag table is auditable: every flag traces to a rule and a value.
  2. The scheduling / orchestration wrapper. The job that loads data on the SLA, runs the checks, packages the evidence, pages the human, emits a heartbeat, and is watched by an independent dead-man’s-switch. This wrapper is validated to the risk it carries (it can fail to page — a safety-relevant failure mode — so the watchdog is part of the validated design).
Validation here is risk-based, proportionate to what the component can get wrong. The screening logic and its scheduler are validated because a missed page or a wrong flag has safety consequences; the presentation viewer is lower-risk because it produces no reportable number and adjudicates nothing — but it still must faithfully render the validated flag table and never invent a value the job did not emit.

Where the validated analysis tools own the answer

The monitoring job and any viewer screen; they never report. The PK/concentration-QTc analysis, the ICH E14 categorical table, central-read ECG intervals, AE incidence tables, the MTD declaration, and final disposition come from the validated tools — Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, and the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline — under the SAP, reviewed by the people accountable for them. The %tm_* job’s job is to get a human to look sooner; it is not the source of the number that goes in the CSR.

For the clinical and regulatory definitions behind every threshold mentioned here, see Clinical & regulatory reference.

Clinical & regulatory reference

This appendix is the standards layer under the dashboard. For each framework it gives a one-line working definition and then draws the line that runs through the whole wiki: what the dashboard does (screen) versus what the regulation or validated analysis owns (determine / report). The dashboard screens against thresholds derived from these standards; it never produces the regulated table, the adjudication, or the reportable number — those belong to the medical monitor, the SRC, pharmacovigilance, and the validated tools (Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline).

FrameworkOne-line definitionWhat the dashboard does (screen)What the regulation / validated analysis owns (determine / report)
Hy’s Law / eDISH A drug-induced liver-injury warning pattern: hepatocellular injury (ALT/AST ≥3×ULN) with concurrent total bilirubin ≥2×ULN and no better explanation (e.g. cholestasis). eDISH is the evaluation-of-Drug-Induced-Serious-Hepatotoxicity scatter (peak ALT vs peak TBili, log scale). Plots the eDISH scatter and fires a deterministic AND-gate screening flag when both the ALT and the ≥2× TBili arms cross at a visit. Uses only the approved language — “potential Hy’s-Law quadrant”, “Hy’s-Law screening position”, “AND-gate position pending confirmation.” Shows the R-ratio to flag cholestatic confounding (e.g. CP101-014’s MIXED pattern) and keeps the near-miss CP101-009 GREEN. Never calls a subject or region a “Hy’s-Law case.” Confirmation requires a repeat draw, R-ratio/ALP cholestasis exclusion, and ruling out viral/biliary/alcohol/concomitant-drug/underlying causes — a medical-monitor determination. Hy’s-Law logic is only entertained after that workup.
ICH E14 (QTc) The regulatory framework for assessing drug effects on cardiac repolarization, including categorical-outlier thresholds (QTc >450/480/500 ms; ΔQTc >30/60 ms) using a pre-specified rate correction. Renders per-visit QTcF (Fridericia) trajectories with a cohort-median band and a categorical-outlier census (counts by cohort), explicitly labelled “SCREENING CENSUS — NOT THE ICH E14 TABLE.” Bands a value RED/AMBER/WATCH; marks baseline-watch points (e.g. CP101-018 at 462 ms baseline) as not a drug effect. Owns the actual ICH E14 categorical table, central-read ECG intervals, and any concentration-QTc / exposure-response analysis — produced by the validated tools (Phoenix WinNonlin) under the SAP, not by this dashboard.
CTCAE v5 NCI Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events — standardized severity grading (Grade 1–5) for AEs and lab abnormalities. Applies CTCAE-style screening bands to single lab values (lab-shift grid, hematology), capping Hgb at Grade 3 since clinical Grade 4 needs monitor assessment. Labels these a deterministic SCREENING BAND, not an adjudicated AE grade. Keeps severity separate from seriousness; Grade-5 is absent by design. Owns the adjudicated AE grade (an investigator/clinical assessment of the event, not a single value) and the reported AE incidence-by-grade tables — from the validated pipeline. A Grade-3 AE is not automatically serious; seriousness is a separate ICH determination.
3+3 dose-escalation design A classic rule-based escalation: enroll 3, expand to 6 on 1 DLT, declare the dose intolerable / MTD-exceeded at ≥2 DLTs in a cohort; DLTs assessed within a defined window. Computes the 3+3 state per cohort from the recorded DLT count (planned N read from the design — C3 was designed N=6, never “expanded”) and shows a trigger / decision-pending label (e.g. C4’s 2/6 = MTD-exceeded TRIGGER). DLT window = 28 days. Never escalates, expands, de-escalates, or declares MTD. DLT adjudication and every escalation decision are made and recorded by the SRC; the dashboard only displays the recorded DLT status and the computed state.
FDA DILI premarketing guidance FDA guidance on detecting and evaluating drug-induced liver injury in premarketing clinical studies (close monitoring of ALT/AST/TBili/ALP, repeat testing, alternative-cause workup). Operationalizes the monitoring posture: per-visit LFT trajectories (ALT/AST/TBili/ALP/GGT), the eDISH view, time-to-peak and trend, and a prompt to obtain a confirmatory repeat — i.e. it tells a human when to look harder. Owns the causality assessment, the alternative-cause exclusion, the dechallenge/rechallenge interpretation, and any decision to discontinue — clinical determinations by the medical monitor, informed by but not made by the screen.
R-ratio (CIOMS) R = (ALT/ULN) ÷ (ALP/ULN), the CIOMS pattern classifier: hepatocellular ≥5, mixed 2–5, cholestatic ≤2. Computes R per subject and shows a pattern strip (hepatocellular / mixed / cholestatic), with an “unstable near ULN” guard when values sit too close to ULN for the ratio to be meaningful. Helps distinguish a hepatocellular signal from cholestasis (CP101-014 reads R≈4.0 = MIXED, not hepatocellular). Owns the clinical interpretation of the pattern and its role in the Hy’s-Law / DILI workup — R-ratio cholestasis exclusion is one input the medical monitor weighs, not a determination the dashboard makes.
CKD-EPI 2021 eGFR The 2021 race-free CKD-EPI creatinine equation estimating glomerular filtration rate (mL/min/1.73m²). Shows a renal panel: creatinine fold-rise over the subject’s own baseline plus a CKD-EPI-2021 eGFR estimate for context. Labelled an estimate; it is a screening context value, never a tier on its own. Owns any reported renal-function analysis and clinical interpretation of renal change — the eGFR estimate is illustrative context, not a validated reportable result.
The through-line. Across every row, the dashboard screens — it prompts a human to look, using thresholds derived from these standards on synthetic data. It never determines, adjudicates, or reports. The regulated table, the causality call, the AE grade, the MTD, and the reported number are owned by the medical monitor, the SRC, pharmacovigilance, and the validated tools (Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the validated SDTM/ADaM pipeline). For the rules that enforce this everywhere, see The governance model; for how it maps to the live job, see From demo to production.

Standards summarized for screening-design context; consult the current ICH, FDA, NCI, and CIOMS source documents for authoritative definitions and any version updates. All data in the dashboard is synthetic and illustrative.

Exploratory ▸ trial-structure analytics (persistence homology)

The console's eighth tab is deliberately set apart from the seven validated views. It is exploratory structural analysis — unsupervised persistence homology over the cohort — offered for hypothesis generation, never for a determination. It produces no flag, no number, and no escalation; nothing here can change study status. This page explains what it computes, how to read each panel, and the hard line that keeps it separate from the safety backbone.

Not part of the validated safety backbone. Everything in the other seven sections is pre-specified, deterministic, validated threshold logic. This tab is none of those things: it is an unsupervised view of how subjects sit relative to one another in safety-feature space. Treat it as a conversation-starter for a human, not as a check. It never fires, never reports a number, and never decides — the medical monitor, SRC and covering biostatistician own every call exactly as before.

What it computes

Each subject is placed in a standardized 10-feature safety space — peak ALT, AST, total bilirubin, ALP, max QTcF, ΔQTcF, nadir haemoglobin and ANC, peak creatinine, and TEAE count — each feature z-scored so no single scale dominates. On that point cloud it runs the Vietoris–Rips H₀ filtration (single-linkage): grow a connection radius ε from zero, subjects link into components, and components merge as ε grows. H₀ (“Betti-zero”) tracks how many separate components survive at each radius. A subject that stays in its own component until a large radius is a topological outlier — far from everyone else in safety-feature space.

Why H₀, and why in-browser. H₀ (connected components / clustering) is the rigorous, interpretable part for “how do the subjects group” — it is exact single-linkage, computed in the browser on the same seeded cohort the rest of the dashboard uses, so the picture can never disagree with the validated panels' data. Higher-dimensional persistence (H₁ loops, voids) in a production build is precomputed offline with ripser / GUDHI; it is intentionally not shown here, to avoid over-claiming on a 22-subject synthetic cohort.

The four panels, and how to read them

PanelWhat it showsHow to read it
PCA embeddingA 2-D PCA projection of the 10-feature space; each dot a subject, coloured by its H₀ cluster at the chosen radius ε*.Dots that sit apart from the main cloud are structurally unusual. A red ring marks a subject that already carries a deterministic screening flag — watch where those land.
β₀ curveThe number of components remaining as the radius ε grows, from n down to 1.A long flat plateau means a stable cluster structure; the dashed ε* line marks where the largest gap defines the natural number of subpopulations.
H₀ barcodeOne horizontal bar per component, length = how long it survived before merging.The longest bars are the most-separated subjects — the topological outliers. Short bars are subjects that joined the crowd early.
Site networkSynthetic sites positioned by their subjects' mean location in feature space; an edge joins similar sites; node size = enrolment.A red-ringed node is a site holding a flagged subject. H₀ of this graph = how many disconnected groups of sites exist — how fragmented enrolment is in safety-feature space.

The one genuinely useful read

The subjects persistence homology isolates as outliers — the longest H₀ bars, the dots sitting apart in the PCA view — turn out to be the same subjects the deterministic checks already flag (the Hy’s-Law and QTcF REDs). That agreement is the point worth noticing: an independent, unsupervised method arrives at the same “these subjects are unusual” conclusion as the pre-specified thresholds, by a completely different route. It is a cross-check on the structure — reassuring when it agrees, worth a human glance when it disagrees — but never a second opinion that decides anything.

What this view cannot do, by construction. It is sensitive to which features you include and how you scale them — change the feature set and the clusters move. It has no notion of a threshold, a protocol, or a diagnosis. It cannot tell you a subject is safe or unsafe; it can only tell you a subject is near or far from the others in the particular space you chose. So it generates hypotheses for a human to pursue with the validated tools — it never closes one. That is exactly why it is fenced off in its own tab with its own banner.

A note on the experimental engine: a separate research-grade predictive engine is not included here and is not part of this package. Persistence homology, used this way as an honest structural summary, is a mature method; the predictive engine is not, and it stays out.
